item.title: Analysis finds 37% of German parliamentarians have additional income
item.description: More than a third of the current members of the lower house of the German parliament have earned additional income, according to an analysis published on Monday. The figure rises to 50% for Bundestag members from Chancellor Friedrich Merz's conservative CDU/CSU alliance, according to the analysis of compulsory reporting figures compiled by Der Spiegel news magazine and the Abgeordneten-Watch (Lawmaker Watch) website.

item.title: Study: Violence and bullying everyday reality at many Berlin schools
item.description: Violence and bullying have become part of everyday life at many Berlin schools, according to a study presented by the education minister in the city's administration, Katharina Günther-Wünsch, who described the findings as a clear warning signal. According to the representative survey for the "Berlin Conflict and Violence Barometer" published on Monday, 38% of teachers and educational staff perceive violence among pupils as a major problem, with 18% describing it as a very major problem.

item.title: Study: One in five young Germans still feels lonely years after Covid
item.description: Young adults in Germany continue to experience loneliness at particularly high rates several years after the Covid pandemic, with just over one in five people aged 21 to 30 saying they feel very lonely, according to a scientific study. Published by the Federal Institute for Population Research (BiB) on Monday to mark Action Week Against Loneliness, the study found that among those aged 31 to 54, the figure was 14%.

item.title: Former Kenyan justice minister blocked from entering Uganda, lawyers' body says
item.description: Martha Karua is a lawyer representing detained Ugandan opposition leader Kizza Besigye who is on trial for treason.

item.title: Ukrainian cruise missile attack sets Russian factory ablaze
item.description: A cruise missile attack has damaged a factory in the south-western Russian city of Voronezh and caused injury to three people, regional governor Alexander Gusev posted on Telegram on Monday. Ukraine's General Staff later confirmed the attack in a statement, saying a factory that produces electronic components for missiles was hit.

item.title: Aoun defends talks as Hezbollah condemns negotiations
item.description: Lebanese President Joseph Aoun said on Monday that Lebanon welcomes international assistance to help end the war between Israel and Hezbollah but rejected any foreign interference in the country's internal affairs. "We are a sovereign state, and no one negotiates on our behalf," Aoun said, according to a statement from the presidency.

item.title: Can China’s latest air-to-air missile take on its US equivalent? Definitely maybe, experts say.
item.description: The PL-16’s specs would let it chase large, slower-moving aircraft, one analyst said, though its effectiveness against smaller, faster planes is unclear.

item.title: Gaza’s surfers seek solace from war in the Mediterranean Sea
item.description: Surfers still ride the waves off Gaza despite the ever-present threat of becoming an Israeli military target.

item.title: Ukraine is showing the West why cheap sensors could be key to defending the skies in future wars
item.description: Ukraine is using a vast network of cheap sensors to detect incoming drone threats. Western officials say the West should consider doing the same.

item.title: US carries out days of airstrikes in Somalia after a month’s pause
item.description: A week of strikes targeted al-Shabab in renewed fighting against the militant group.

item.title: Pope: No single state can address global challenges alone
item.description: Pope Leo XIV warned against an increasingly fragmented world order and a crisis in multilateralism during a visit on Monday to the headquarters of the UN's World Food Programme (WFP) in Rome. Leo called for renewed commitment to multilateral cooperation at a time of crisis.

item.title: Protests erupt in West Bank after two young Palestinians shot dead
item.description: Protests erupted in the occupied West Bank on Monday after the Palestinian Health Ministry reported that two Palestinian males aged 15 and 19 were shot and killed in the town of Beit Ummar. A further two youths were injured in the shooting on Sunday evening in the town lying to the north of Hebron, the report said.

item.title: Colombia's Trump-backed presidential candidate claims victory in nail-biting election
item.description: President Donald Trump had endorsed Abelardo de la Espriella, a political outsider who refers to himself as "The Tiger."

item.title: South Africa to raise health funding cuts at UN meeting
item.description: The meeting comes after the US said it plans to cut HIV/AIDS funding for the country, where rates are the highest in the world.

item.title: Libyan warlord convicted over abuses at detention center, officials say
item.description: A Libyan warlord has been convicted of human rights violations reportedly committed at a detention facility in the country's west, authorities said, over a year after Italy deported him despite an arrest warrant issued by the International Criminal Court. Ossama Anjiem, also known as Ossama al-Masri, who led the Tripoli branch of the Reform and Rehabilitation Institution, was sentenced to seven years and four months in prison for "violating the rights of inmates” who reported “torture, cruelty and degrading treatment,” according to a statement Sunday by the attorney general's office. The facility is part of a network of detention centers run by the government-backed Special Defense Force, or SDF.

item.title: Confirmed Ebola cases in Congo top 1,000, deaths surpass 250, officials say
item.description: Confirmed Ebola cases in the outbreak in eastern Congo have reached 1,003, including 254 deaths, officials said, and tracing those who've been in contact with patients remains a major challenge.

item.title: Russian authorities ban Crimea children's holidays as security worsens
item.description: The Moscow-appointed authorities in Russian-occupied Crimea on Monday banned taking children on holiday to the Black Sea peninsula up to September 1 in response to the current security situation. "These measures are necessary for the maintenance of public safety," Sergey Aksyonov, the Moscow-appointed political head of Crimea, said on Telegram.

item.title: Russia withdraws air defences from front line to protect Moscow
item.description: Russia has pulled an air defence system from the front line and moved it to Moscow to defend the Russian capital.

item.title: Philippines DICT taps Google Cloud for enterprise AI in government
item.description: DICT and Google Cloud will deploy AI tools and boost cybersecurity across Philippine government digital services and infrastructure.

item.title: Major raids target Europe-wide drug ring operating from Germany
item.description: Investigators have moved against a group of suspected drug traffickers in the German state of North Rhine-Westphalia, Spain and Austria, seizing more than 4 metric tons of cannabis along with kilograms of cocaine and crack cocaine, the Cologne public prosecutor's office said on Monday. Acting on behalf of the Cologne prosecutor's office, investigators searched 31 private homes, business premises and hiding places.

item.title: Human Remains Found on Remote Island Identified as Missing Dad Who Disappeared Nearly 3 Years Ago on Kayaking Trip
item.description: Samuel McAlister went missing in New Zealand in September 2023

item.title: Daily Briefing: Is the Strait open or closed?
item.description: The latest on end-of-war talks with Iran. Here is the news to know on Monday.

item.title: Right-wing populist wins Colombia presidential runoff
item.description: Voters across much of Latin America have increasingly favored conservative leaders in the face of rising crime and slowing growth.

item.title: Taiwan's army practises combat readiness with a five-day exercise
item.description: Taiwan on Monday launched a five-day military exercise aimed at enhancing battlefield familiarity and improving the armed forces' ability to rapidly shift from peacetime to wartime operations, amid ongoing tensions with China. According to Taiwan's state-run Central News Agency (CNA), combat units began deploying early Monday morning to designated defensive positions across the island in preparation for a potential enemy invasion.

item.title: 3 dead, 7 wounded in rare Philippines school shooting, police say
item.description: Two students with hand guns opened fire in a high school in the central Philippines, killing three fellow students and wounding another seven, police said.

item.title: Record stash of cocaine found buried underground in Australia
item.description: Police discovered 3 tons of cocaine in plastic tubs buried in underground bunkers that were concealed by false floors.

item.title: UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer Officially Calls It Quits
item.description: Keir Starmer, who has had a tumultuous run as leader of the U.K.'s Labour Party, announced on Monday that he will step down after less than two years at 10 Downing Street.

item.title: Left party leader apologizes for calling German conservatives fascists
item.description: The new leader of Germany's The Left party, Luigi Pantisano, has apologized for his remarks likening the policies of the Christian Democratic Union (CDU) to fascism, telling dpa his statement had been "shortened and wrong in that form. " "My statement that there is currently no difference between the policies of the CDU and the AfD was shortened and wrong in that form," Pantisano told dpa on Monday, referring to the anti-immigrant Alternative for Germany party.

item.title: Syria rules out military role in Lebanon, open to Hezbollah dialogue
item.description: Syrian interim President Ahmed al-Sharaa said Syria remains opposed to any military involvement in Lebanon and is instead seeking to support stability through political and economic cooperation. In an interview with Al Mashhad television broadcast on Sunday, al-Sharaa said Damascus had been in contact with US President Donald Trump as part of efforts to help calm the conflict in Lebanon, stressing that Syria's role should be "positive" and aimed at supporting stability.

item.title: Teenage rapist jailed for attacks on girls in Dunfermline
item.description: Declan Madden committed the crimes in his hometown in Fife when he was aged 14 and 15.

item.title: Three dead in Philippines high school shooting over bullying 'grudge'
item.description: Police say they believe the attack was motivated by a "grudge" over bullying.

item.title: No German, no swim: Language entry rule sparks debate
item.description: A lakeside beach in the central German city of Halle has introduced a controversial rule barring visitors without sufficient German language skills from entering, citing safety concerns, its manager told dpa on Monday. "We need to be sure that visitors understand our bathing rules and be consistent enough to guarantee the safety of our guests," said Mathias Nobel, manager of the Heidebad lake swimming area in Halle.

item.title: Canadian town officially recognizes trees as living beings with rights: ‘Our biggest ally’
item.description: The move was inspired by Quebec filmmaker André Desrocher who said ‘a tree is like a human being’

item.title: UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer resigns
item.description: Starmer was deeply unpopular, with personal favorability ratings of -42.

item.title: High School Shooting Leaves 3 Dead and 7 Injured After 2 Students Allegedly Launch Attack Following Bullying
item.description: The incident took place in the central Philippines on Monday, June 22, at around 9 a.m. local time

item.title: Trump-backed populist wins tight Colombia presidential vote, sparking protests
item.description: A flamboyant Trump-backed lawyer who's never held public office narrowly won Colombia's polarizing presidential runoff, swinging the country hard right and sparking violent protests.

item.title: 'Eye-shaped' crop circle found in farmer's field
item.description: The circle, described by a photographer as "spectacular", has since been removed.

item.title: Syria rules out military role in Lebanon, open to Hezbollah dialogue
item.description: Syrian interim President Ahmed al-Sharaa said Syria remains opposed to any military involvement in Lebanon and is instead seeking to support stability through political and economic cooperation. In an interview with Al Mashhad television broadcast on Sunday, al-Sharaa said Damascus had been in contact with US President Donald Trump as part of efforts to help calm the conflict in Lebanon, stressing that Syria's role should be "positive" and aimed at supporting stability.

item.title: Gaza's surfers seek solace in the sea
item.description: Carrying their boards past tents and bombed-out buildings, a group of Gazan surfers headed for the sea, seeking solace in the waves despite the risk of Israeli attacks.A few children splashed in the shallows as the surfers paddled out to sea, fighting the crashing waves.

item.title: Nearly one million Gazans remain in tents amid extreme summer heat, Norwegian report suggests
item.description: The Norwegian Refugee Council notes that it leads “the Shelter Cluster in Palestine, which coordinates humanitarian shelter actors responding to emergency shelter needs in Gaza and the West Bank. ” Nearly one million civilians in Gaza continue to live in tents as the summer begins in 2026, according to a new report by the Norwegian Refugee Council.

item.title: ‘Daily cuts… infections’: India’s e-waste workers face toxic health risks
item.description: As digital consumption grows and e-waste mounts, burden of managing that waste falls on workers with little protection.

item.title: Extreme sport deaths expose a patchwork of safety regulations
item.description: Fifteen people died in one weekend, prompting calls for better regulation in a booming area of tourism.

item.title: Ebola cases in the Congo top 1,000 with more than 250 deaths
item.description: Just over a month after the start of the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, the number of confirmed cases in the country has risen to more than 1,000. The Information Ministry in Kinshasa reported that 254 of the 1,003 confirmed Ebola patients in three provinces have died, while 100 people are considered recovered.

item.title: No German, no swim: Language entry rule sparks debate
item.description: A lakeside beach in the central German city of Halle has introduced a controversial rule barring visitors without sufficient German language skills from entering, citing safety concerns, its manager told dpa on Monday. "We need to be sure that visitors understand our bathing rules and be consistent enough to guarantee the safety of our guests," said Mathias Nobel, manager of the Heidebad lake swimming area in Halle.

item.title: Gujarat woman climbs onto cooler after waking to deadly Russell's viper by her bed
item.description: "The snake was trying to climb but was unable to do so."

item.title: A Russian drone strike in Ukraine kills 3 from one family, including a 13-year-old boy
item.description: A Russian drone strike on the city of Sumy in northeastern Ukraine killed three members of the same family, including a 13-year-old boy and his father, and wounded another two, a regional official said Monday. Russia has pounded civilian areas of Ukraine with drones and missiles since it launched its all-out invasion of its neighbor more than four years ago, with a United Nations tally saying more than 16,000 civilians have died in the war. “Their home was destroyed,” Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said on X. “An ordinary home — not a military target whatsoever.”

item.title: Plans to restore 'Merlin's Mound' delayed to 2027
item.description: Plans are pushed back to "ensure the work can be done optimally".

item.title: UK's Starmer expected to set out exit timetable as Burnham waits in the wings
item.description: Source: Reuters

item.title: Former East Timor president and independence fighter Francisco Guterres dies at 71
item.description: Francisco Guterres, a former president of East Timor and a leading figure in the country’s independence movement, has died. Guterres, widely known by his nom de guerre “Lu Olo,” died on Sunday at Prince Court Medical Centre in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, where he had been in intensive care, his family said on the late president's official Facebook account. Guterres served as president from 2017 to 2022, capping decades of involvement in the political and armed struggle that led to independence for Southeast Asia’s youngest nation in 2002.

item.title: Confirmed Ebola cases in Congo outbreak top 1,000 with 254 deaths, authorities say
item.description: Confirmed cases in the Ebola outbreak in eastern Congo have reached 1,003, including 254 deaths, officials said in a statement late Sunday. A total of 100 people have recovered in the outbreak concentrated in the Ituri province since it was declared on May 15, Congo’s Ministry of Health said. The Ebola outbreak caused by the rare Bundibugyo virus, which has no vaccines or treatment, was the worst ever in its first month.

item.title: Australia and Canada sign a $1.75B deal to build long-range radar in Canada
item.description: Australia and Canada signed a $1.75 billion export agreement on Monday to build an Australian-designed long-range radar system in Canada. Australian Defense Minister Richard Marles and Canadian Secretary of State (Defense Procurement) Stephen Fuhr signed the first phase of a pact to provide early warning radar coverage from the Canada-United States border into the Arctic. “What this really means is that Australia and Canada are now partners in terms of the future development of the Over-the-Horizon Radar,” Marles told reporters at the Australian Parliament House in the capital Canberra.

item.title: Colombian protests vow 'resistance' to right-wing rule
item.description: Thousands of leftist Colombians spilled onto the streets of Cali and Bogota on Sunday to protest hard-right president-elect Abelardo de la Espriella's narrow election win.De la Espriella's bombastic electoral campaign divided Colombia, with his supporter base eagerly awaiting a return to right-wing governance after the country's first leftist president Gustavo Petro's four-year stint in office.

item.title: 'It's our duty': The health frontliners training to tackle Ebola
item.description: Joseph Emuron watches closely as his colleagues from several East African countries slowly leave the "red zone" during their pandemic preparedness training in a teaching hospital in Nairobi. It was only in April that the tall Ugandan doctor in his blue shrubs had learned the process of "doffing," the safe removal of the protective suit that can be the last barrier between a medic and a deadly virus when treating a patient with Ebola.

item.title: 3-D printer warriors across Europe help Ukraine fight Russia
item.description: Ukraine has been defending itself from a war Russia started more than four years ago - but it also has an army working for it across Europe. DrukArmy, or PrintArmy, is the name of a volunteer organization whose activists mass-produce small plastic parts using 3D printers.

item.title: Ukraine is squeezing Crimea with its new drones, forcing the Russian stronghold to cut fuel supplies
item.description: Kyiv's new mid-range drones are pounding the roads, fuel depots, and bridges connecting Crimea to Russia and the southern front lines.

item.title: Operator dismisses 'speculative and unverified' ticket prices for Malaysian rail link
item.description: Fares for Malaysia's long-awaited East Coast Rail Link (ECRL) have not yet been announced, contrary to social media posts sharing a graphic they claim shows ticket prices for stops on the route. The railway's operator says prices circulating online are "speculative and unverified" and do not show the actual fares that will be imposed when the rail link opens in January 2027. The graphic accompanying the posts also contains spelling mistakes and lists purported destinations that are not part of t

item.title: Africa faces child surgery crisis as key anaesthesia runs out
item.description: African hospitals face huge new costs as they scramble to replace the main anaesthetic for child operations that is set to run out in 2027, a charity has warned.The transition has exposed years of under-funding in anaesthesia systems on the continent, with many discovering that machines need to be replaced at a cost of tens of thousands of dollars each.

item.title: Upskirting crimes have long plagued Japan. Now children are becoming offenders
item.description: Despite years of efforts to curb the crime, upskirting remains one of Japan’s most common sex offenses. But what’s changing is who’s committing it.

item.title: Congo says confirmed Ebola cases rise over 1,000
item.description: June 22 () - The Democratic ‌Republic ‌of Congo said ​late on Sunday that confirmed ‌Ebola ⁠cases in the ⁠country had ​risen ​to 1,003, ​including ‌254 deaths. In an update on Saturday, the ‌government ​put ​confirmed ​cases ‌at 956 and ​deaths ​at 247.

item.title: Europe Today: Inside the G7 Summit in Évian
item.description: Global leaders are gathering in the French spa town of Évian les Bains for the second day of the G7. US President Donald Trump told allies that he expects the Strait of Hormuz to be “fully open” on Friday, where the framework deal to end the war is expected to be formalised in Switzerland.

item.title: Students head to European final with a bananas idea
item.description: The Harper Adams University students are the UK entry for a major entrepreneurship festival in Latvia.

item.title: Tech giants join Prince of Wales for wildlife campaign
item.description: Technology giants including Google, Meta and Amazon will publicly commit to end wildlife trafficking on their platforms at an event convened by the Prince of Wales.

item.title: Trump-backed De La Espriella claims victory in tight Colombia presidential runoff
item.description: A far-right lawyer with the endorsement of U.S. President Donald Trump claimed victory Sunday night in Colombia's presidential runoff election.

item.title: IDF considering handing Lebanese army control of Hezbollah underground compound
item.description: Israel believes that transferring the responsibility to Lebanon’s army could serve as a test of its ability and willingness to act against Hezbollah, said N12. Security officials are considering allowing Lebanon’s army to take control of a Hezbollah tunnel compound in the southern Lebanese village of Tebnit, N12 reported on Sunday, citing sources familiar with the matter.

item.title: High oil prices drive a surge in Chinese electric vehicle sales, but charging networks lag behind
item.description: The war in Iran has helped reshape the global electric vehicle market, giving Chinese automakers an opening across the developing world as soaring fuel prices push drivers towards electric vehicles, even as charging infrastructure lags behind a wave of imports. The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz disrupted shipping of about a fifth of the world’s crude oil and liquified natural gas, first hitting Asia — the main destination for the fuels — followed by Africa. This shock accelerated a trend that was already spreading across the developing world.

item.title: 2 young suspects in custody after shooting at high school in Philippines kills 3
item.description: Two young students opened fire in a high school in the central Philippines on Monday, killing three fellow students and wounding another seven, police said. The suspects, aged 14 and 15, were armed with one pistol each. The suspects and the victims were students of the San Jose National High School in Tacloban city, where the mid-morning shooting happened, regional police chief Brig. Gen. Jason Capoy said.

item.title: Japan quintuples visa fees in first price hike since 1978
item.description: Authorities say they do not expect the hikes to have an "immediate impact on inbound tourism".

item.title: AI and 3D X-rays could catch wildlife smugglers after Argentina airport haul overwhelms rescuers
item.description: "Technology can flag a bag. People, forensics, and prosecutors turn a flagged bag into a sentence."

item.title: Trump-endorsed de la Espriella holds slim lead in Colombia's election as his rival challenges vote
item.description: A polarized Colombia gave conservative political outsider Abelardo de la Espriella a razor-thin lead in a runoff election that will be challenged in the coming days by the ruling party’s progressive candidate. De la Espriella, a business owner and lawyer who earned U.S. President Donald Trump’s endorsement despite never having run for office, led progressive lawmaker Iván Cepeda taking 49.7% of the votes, with 99.9% of results released by electoral authorities. Cepeda, Petro’s ally, earned 48.7% support.

item.title: Australian police uncover 3 tons of cocaine
item.description: Police found 2.7 metric tons (3 tons) of cocaine on a property on Sydney’s outskirts in Australia’s largest ever seizure of the drug, officials said on Monday. The drug was found on June 19 in plastic tubs buried in underground bunkers hidden beneath three shipping containers on a semirural property in the suburb of Londonderry on Sydney’s western edge, the Queensland Joint Organized Crime Taskforce said in a statement. The containers had false floors that provided access to the cocaine, which police estimate had a street value of 816 million Australian dollars ($572 million).

item.title: Australia confirms second H5 bird flu case
item.description: (Corrects name of ‌agriculture minister ‌to Julie ​Collins, not Murray Watt) SYDNEY, ‌June ⁠22 () - Australia's agriculture ⁠minister Julie ​Collins ​on Monday ​said ‌testing has confirmed a second case ‌of ​H5 ​bird ​flu ‌in Western Australia. (Reporting ​by ​Christine Chen ​in ‌Sydney; Editing ​by Alasdair ​Pal).

item.title: Trump-backed de la Espriella edges ahead in Colombia presidential vote
item.description: Right-wing lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella, who received backing from US President Donald Trump, appeared headed for a razor-thin victory in Colombia's presidential election on Sunday, according to preliminary results. With 99.

item.title: Police find 400 live cats, 80 dead in Vietnam cat meat ring, and 40 make it back home
item.description: "Many families were looking for their cats and didn’t find them."

item.title: Australia sells advanced radar technology to Canada in record $1.7 billion deal
item.description: Australia said on Monday it will sell advanced radar technology capable of detecting long-range ‌missiles to Canada under a A$2.5 billion ($1.75 billion) ‌agreement, the country's largest-ever defence export deal. The agreement is Australia's first overseas sale ​of the radar, known as Over-the-Horizon Radar technology, and will support Canada's surveillance of the Arctic region. "Today's agreement marks a significant milestone in Australian defence trade and lays the foundation for ‌deeper and mutually beneficial ⁠defence industry collaboration with Canada," Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said in a statement.

item.title: Shipping stalls in Strait of Hormuz after Iran declares key waterway shut
item.description: Ship tracking data shows sharp fall in transits as US and Iranian officials hold talks to save fragile peace framework.

item.title: How Ukraine's Drone Diplomacy Is Strengthening Ties With Azerbaijan
item.description: Ukraine is leveraging drone warfare expertise and defense innovation to deepen ties with Azerbaijan and expand its diplomatic influence beyond the battlefield.

item.title: Explosion as Qatar restarts gas export terminal hurts 54 and leaves 18 missing
item.description: An explosion tore through Qatar's key natural gas export terminal Sunday night as workers tried to resume operations there after Iran bombed it during the war, causing a fire that hurt at least 54 people as another 18 were still missing hours later. The blast at the Ras Laffan industrial area could cause further chaos in global energy markets, particularly as Qatar remains one of the world's top natural gas producers. Qatar shut down its production after Iran's chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz meant it couldn't get shipments out to its clients.

item.title: Hard-Right millionaire lawyer wins Colombia’s presidential election
item.description: Abelardo de la Espriella, the hard-Right millionaire lawyer and self-proclaimed “outsider”, has won Colombia’s presidential race, according to the initial vote count.

item.description: IDF forces then began scanning the area for additional threats, as firefighters work to extinguish fires resulting from the tossed Molotovs and burning tires. The IDF killed two terrorists who threw Molotov cocktails near Karmei Tzur in the West Bank during a targeted counterterrorism operation Sunday night, the military announced.

item.description: The Sundarbans remains an extraordinary ecosystem, but it is also a working landscape for nearby residents trying to make a living.

item.description: Abelardo De la Espriella was endorsed by Trump earlier this month.

item.description: SEOUL, June 22 (Reuters) - Two vessels operated by South Korea passed through the Strait of Hormuz after the U.S. and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding on a ceasefire agreement last week,

item.title: Family of California woman Ashley Phillips who went missing in Costa Rica shares tragic update
item.description: The body of a California woman who went missing in a remote area of Costa Rica earlier this month has been confirmed to be 30-year-old Ashley Phillips.

item.title: Lioness chasing a dog plunges into an open well in India, trapping both for 5 hours
item.description: Forest officials said the rescue operation took careful consideration to ensure both animals were safe.

item.description: "They were paddling in circles out in the bay, drifting further away,” the teen said while recounting the rescue

item.description: Police announced that Tallulah Bradford had died, one week after being injured in the incident

item.title: Israel will not withdraw troops from southern Lebanon ahead of negotiations, official tells 'Post'
item.description: An earlier report from LBCI claimed that the US had notified Lebanon that Israel was planning to withdraw some troops as a “goodwill gesture” ahead of Israel-Lebanon negotiations. A senior Israeli official told The Jerusalem Post on Saturday that the IDF will not be withdrawing from Lebanon or making concessions, contrary to Lebanese reports stating the opposite.

item.title: Is Germany looking again at coal-powered electricity?
item.description: It had planned to abandon the fuel, but the higher cost of natural gas may make it think again.

item.description: Tech companies and authorities are failing women by focusing on nudity rather than consent, says a report by Chayn.

item.description: All told, the ACCC alleges that only about 4% of purchases met the full set of conditions.

item.title: Documenting the horrors of Mauthausen concentration camp
item.description: At 22, Army medic LeRoy "Pete" Petersohn helped liberate the Nazi concentration camp Mauthausen and documented its horrors in a letter home, testimony his son says Petersohn felt compelled to record for history.

item.title: UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer under pressure to resign
item.description: Starmer is said to be weighing “political realities” as Labour members back challenger Andy Burnham.

item.description: Right wing candidate Abelardo De La Espriella faced off with leftist Ivan Cepeda in a tight race to be Colombia's next president.

item.title: Colombia elects Trump-endorsed businessman in presidential runoff
item.description: The polarized nation headed to the polls amid its worst security crisis in decades.

item.title: Home Front Command eases security guideline restrictions on Lebanon border communities
item.description: Due to the long-standing conflict with the Lebanese terrorist group Hezbollah, many border communities have been restricted throughout the conflict. Defensive guidelines in areas near Lebanon will be restored to a level of “full activity ” on Monday, the IDF announced in a statement Sunday.

item.title: Taiwan announces combat readiness drill
item.description: Taiwan’s defense ministry on Sunday announced a five-day combat readiness exercise as China intensifies military activity around the island.

item.title: Ukraine attacks trigger Russian fuel shortages
item.description: Ukraine is hammering Russia’s oil infrastructure, triggering fuel shortages and increasing gas prices.

item.title: Far-right wind power protesters occupy Germanic war monument
item.description: Members of a far-right anti-immigration movement in Germany occupied a well-known Germanic war monument on Sunday, setting off fireworks and hanging up banners in protest against the expansion of wind power, according to police. The far-right, ethnonationalist Identitarian Movement, which sees white European identity as under threat from immigration, staged the action at the Hermann Memorial near the western German city of Detmold.

item.title: Campers wake to screams as jackals attack 11 on Sea of Galilee beach
item.description: "I quickly turned on the light, and her whole face was covered in blood."

item.title: Ramiro Valdés Menéndez, a key figure of the Cuban Revolution and ally of Fidel Castro, has died
item.description: Ramiro Valdés Menéndez, a former vice president and a key figure of the Cuban Revolution who fought alongside Fidel Castro, died Sunday, the Communist Party and Cuba’s government said. Valdés was one of the closest allies of Fidel and his brother Raúl Castro since the 1953 attack on the Moncada barracks that marked the beginning of the Cuban Revolution.

item.title: Colombia votes in runoff pitting leftist reformer against law-and-order newcomer
item.description: Source: Reuters

item.title: Netanyahu speaks at JNS summit
item.description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu delivered a speech at the Jerusalem News Syndicate (JNS) on Sunday. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said that Israel prevented Iran from "carrying out a plan to annihilate us" during a speech at the Jerusalem News Syndicate (JNS) International Policy Summit on Sunday.

item.title: Bolivia clears anti-government roadblocks after emergency decree, as patrol plane crash kills 6
item.description: Anti-government roadblocks that have isolated Bolivia’s capital La Paz and other major cities were still being cleared Sunday, a day after President Rodrigo Paz declared a state of emergency that was later overwhelmingly ratified by parliament. Also Sunday, six people, including Bolivian Air Force officers, were killed in the crash of a light aircraft that had been conducting aerial patrols over a blockaded highway. The aircraft had been monitoring sections of the highway linking La Paz and the city of Cochabamba.

item.title: IDF, Shin Bet kill PIJ terrorist who kidnapped minor from kibbutz during October 7 massacre
item.description: PIJ terrorist Zaki Youssef Mahmoud Abu Mustafa was killed in a strike in the Gaza Strip on Friday, according to a joint statement released by the IDF and Shin Bet. The IDF and Shin Bet (Israel Security Agency) announced on Sunday that they killed a Palestinian Islamic Jihad (PIJ) terrorist who participated in the kidnapping of then-12-year-old Yagil Yaakov from Kibbutz Nir Oz during the October 7 massacre.

item.title: Germany ready to conscript soldiers for Lithuania brigade if needed
item.description: The German military is prepared to conscript soldiers for its new armoured brigade in Lithuania if necessary, Defence Minister Boris Pistorius said on Sunday. Germany is establishing a permanent brigade in Lithuania, a member of the European Union and NATO, in response to a perceived growing threat from Russia.

item.title: Syria president denies wanting to intervene in Lebanon after Trump remarks

item.title: Legendary Cuban spy chief Ramiro Valdes dies at 94
item.description: The founder of Cuba's feared intelligence services, Ramiro Valdes, one of the last commanders of Fidel Castro's revolution, has died at the age of 94, President Miguel Diaz-Canel said Sunday.As interior minister in the 1960s, he set up what would become Cuba's feared G2 state security intelligence service.

item.title: Mourners gather in Beirut to pay respects to Lebanese conservationist who died after Israeli strike
item.description: Mourners gathered Sunday in Beirut to pay their respects to a much-loved Lebanese conservationist who died after succumbing to wounds sustained in an Israeli strike on her home on the country’s southern coast. Mona Khalil, who spent more than two decades protecting sea turtles along Lebanon’s coastline, was critically injured in the strike on her home in the village of Mansouri earlier this month and died of her wounds Friday. The Orange House, which Khalil helped build into a small conservation hub and ecotourism site in Mansouri, became a refuge for endangered loggerhead and green sea turtles and a training ground for volunteers documenting nesting activity along the coast.

item.description: An overwhelming majority of 92% of Israelis believe that Iran has emerged as the winner from the war launched by the United States and Israel in late February, according to a poll conducted by the Hebrew University and published on Sunday. According to the survey, conducted in cooperation with the Agam Institute, 83% believe that the campaign weakened Israel's long-term security, while 86% described the fallout from the war as negative.

As a result of the incident on June 19, 2025, the hospital lost 144 inpatient beds, though the building had been evacuated of patients a day earlier.

item.description: Officials in Russia-occupied Crimea suspended civilian gasoline sales Sunday as Ukraine ramped up attacks on fuel supplies on the Black Sea peninsula. Gov. Sergey Aksyonov, the Kremlin-appointed head o Crimea, said that overnight Ukrainian strikes killed four people and wounded 28 others. “Fuel will be sold only to government agencies that ensure the functioning and security of the Republic of Crimea,” Aksyonov said.

item.description: The Belgian capital will remove rental e-scooters from its streets from January 2027, citing rising accident rates, pavement clutter and their increasing use by organised crime groups. Shared bikes and e-bikes will remain available instead.

item.description: Four people were killed and fuel sales were suspended in Russian-occupied Crimea, the Moscow-backed authorities there said on Sunday, after a massive Ukrainian barrage hit the Black Sea peninsula. The mass raid also killed one person on a ferry and hit an oil terminal in Russia's southern Krasnodar region bordering Crimea, the authorities there said.

item.title: Leftist senator faces Trump-backed rival in Colombia presidential vote
item.description: Voters in Colombia go to the polls on Sunday to decide whether a left-wing politician or a right-wing lawyer will succeed President Gustavo Petro. Around 41 million eligible voters will choose between Senator Iván Cepeda of the governing Historic Pact (Pacto Historico) alliance and right-wing lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella of the Defence of the Homeland (Defensores de la Patria ) movement in a run-off election.

item.title: Exhumation in Indonesia misrepresented as Muslim grave destruction in India
item.description: After Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi's Hindu-nationalist Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) won a landslide victory in the West Bengal elections in May 2026, reports of violence against the state's Muslim community fuelled misinformation online. The latest claim shared a video falsely alleging it showed the destruction of a Muslim's grave in India. In fact, the footage shows authorities clearing an old graveyard in Indonesia to make way for new burials.

item.title: Eight children injured in German motorway accident involving minibus
item.description: Thirteen people, including eight children, have been injured in a motorway accident in south-western Germany, a police spokeswoman said on Sunday. The injured were ferried to hospital by helicopter from the crash site near the small town of Kirchardt.

item.title: Colombians vote in a presidential runoff that pits an outsider against a progressive
item.description: A deeply divided electorate will choose Colombia’s next president in a runoff on Sunday that pits a progressive against a conservative outsider, with both candidates tapping into fears of a renewed internal conflict in the country. Voters will choose between businessman and lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella and Iván Cepeda, a lawmaker and heir to the political movement of outgoing President Gustavo Petro, the nation’s first leftist leader. De la Espriella is proposing a heavy-handed approach that has earned him the endorsement of U.S. President Donald Trump.

The council called on the RSF not to launch an attack on the city, which lies in a strategic area linking central and western Sudan.

item.title: Ubisoft Co-Founder Claude Guillemot Killed in Plane Crash
item.description: Claude Guillemot -- the co-founder of the popular video game company Ubisoft -- has died after a plane he was piloting crashed. According to French media outlets, Guillemot was flying a Cessna 421 twin engine plane which crashed near the beach resort of La Baule in Western France. Guillemot and the instructor were reportedly flying from Rennes in the northwest part of the country to La Baule.

item.title: Iran to close Strait of Hormuz due to Israeli attacks on Lebanon
item.description: Iran's military on Saturday said it will again close the Strait of Hormuz to all shipping, citing alleged Israeli violations of a ceasefire with Hezbollah in Lebanon that formed part of a recent framework deal between Tehran and Washington. In a statement reported in Iranian media, the military also pointed to Israel's continued presence in southern Lebanon and said shutting the key waterway for the global oil trade was "the first step" in responding to the "enemy's breach of trust.

item.title: JD Vance Claims ‘Things Are Actually Getting Better’ in Lebanon Despite Reports of 16 Dead in Overnight Strikes
item.description: J.D. Vance left for Switzerland Saturday evening, but not before gaggling with reporters on the tarmac and declaring that the situation in Lebanon is improving.

item.title: Three men killed following fire in west London
item.description: The men were rescued from a pavilion in White City but died shortly after, authorities said.

item.title: Caribbean Resort Worker Recalls Chaos After Deadly Fire Broke Out, Forcing Guests to Evacuate
item.description: A 46-year-old woman died in the fire, and nine other people required medical treatment

item.title: Zelenskiy again urges Belarus to dismantle relay stations
item.description: June 20 () - Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy urged authorities in neighbouring Belarus for the second day running to dismantle relay stations he said were playing ‌a role in staging Russian drone attacks on Ukrainian regions. Belarus, under longtime ‌President Alexander Lukashenko, has been one of Moscow's closest allies in the more than four-year-old war against Ukraine ​and allowed the Kremlin to use its territory to launch the February 2022 invasion.

item.title: Police Find Body of an Infant amid Search for 3-Month-Old Boy Kidnapped from Stroller While His Mom Was Bringing in Groceries
item.description: “It could be the missing boy. Police operations to identify them are ongoing,” police said in a statement

item.description: The Israeli military accused Ahmed Wishah of being a "Hamas sniper operative", without providing evidence.

item.title: Spanish prime minister’s wife to face corruption trial, judge orders her to surrender passport
item.description: The wife of Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez was ordered by a judge on Saturday to surrender her passport ahead of an impending trial on corruption charges, according to multiple outlets. Begoña Gómez is accused of using her position to help certain private-sector companies secure government contracts and misusing public funds. The Associated Press reported…

item.title: Zelenskiy warns of impending massive Russian attack on Ukraine
item.description: June 20 () - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Saturday that Russian forces were preparing an impending massive attack on Ukraine and ‌warned residents to take special care as Russian strikes in ‌different regions killed at least six people. "Tonight and in the coming hours, it is especially ​important to pay close attention to air raid warnings," Zelenskiy said in his nightly video address.

item.title: Congo says confirmed Ebola cases rise to 956, including 247 deaths
item.description: KINSHASA, June 20 () - ‌The Democratic ‌Republic of ​Congo said on Saturday ‌the ⁠number of confirmed ⁠Ebola cases ​in ​the country ​had ‌risen to 956, including 247 deaths. On ‌Friday, ​confirmed ​cases ​were ‌at 933, and ​deaths ​were 245.

item.title: 5 men stabbed in alleged anti-Muslim attack in Edinburgh, Scotland
item.description: Police arrested a man after stabbing five people in Edinburgh, Scotland, in suspected anti-Muslim attacks. A 36-year-old man was arrested.

item.title: Haridwar family is left shocked after 27 baby snakes are found in household water tank
item.description: The reaction has largely been a mix of disbelief and anxiety.

item.title: Air Force relocates recon drone squadron to Japan
item.description: Three of the Air Force’s large RQ-4 Global Hawk drones will be permanently stationed in Yokota Air Base.

item.title: Mona Khalil, 76, sea turtle advocate, dies from Israeli attack
item.description: Lebanese conservationist and protector of sea turtles Mona Khalil died from injuries she suffered when her home was hit by an Israeli strike.

item.title: Reports: Israel halts strikes outside Lebanon 'security zone'
item.description: The Israeli military has halted strikes in Lebanon outside a self-declared "security zone" along the border, according to Israeli media reports citing the armed forces. The move follows instructions from Israel's political leadership, the reports said.

item.title: Bukele says El Salvador makes record cocaine seizure
item.description: El Salvador has seized a record amount of cocaine in its latest anti-drug operations, President Nayib Bukele said on Saturday. A total of 6.

item.title: Co-founder of 'Assassin's Creed' company Ubisoft killed in plane crash
item.description: Two people were killed when a plane crashed in western France, including a co-founder of video game giant Ubisoft, authorities and the company said on Saturday. The twin-motor plane crashed into a field near the town of La Baule on Friday evening while approaching the local airfield, according to the public prosecutor's office.

item.title: Bolivian president declares state of emergency after weeks of protests
item.description: Bolivian President Rodrigo Paz has declared a nationwide state of emergency after more than 50 days of protests and road blockades that have disrupted transport and daily life across the country. In a televised address to the nation on Saturday, Paz said Bolivians could no longer be held "hostage" by the blockades that were preventing people from working, studying, accessing medical care and supporting their families.

item.title: ‘Unsynchronized Diplomatic Process’: Former Obama Official Questions Why Lebanon and Israel Aren’t At Peace Talks
item.description: A former Obama State department official questioned Saturday why Israel and Lebanon aren't at the peace talks in Switzerland, along with the U.S. and Iran.

item.title: Iran closes Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz once again over Israeli “violations” of the peace deal agreed with the US, Tehran’s military has said.

item.title: The unanswered questions over the Bedford railway crash
item.description: The number of apparent failures that led to the train crash near Bedford on Friday has left experts baffled.

item.title: Opinion - Chinese firms can’t get away with abusing workers overseas
item.description: Overseas labor conditions are becoming a genuine constraint on Chinese outbound investment.

item.title: Zelensky warns of new large-scale Russian attacks, urges precautions
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky warned on Saturday evening that Russia was preparing new large-scale attacks and urged citizens to take precautions. Speaking in his nightly video address to the nation, Zelensky pointed to strikes on the cities of Dnipro and Zaporizhzhya hours earlier, saying that Moscow now appeared to be preparing for more assaults overnight.

item.title: Lebanese turtle conservationist Mona Khalil killed by Israeli strike
item.description: Mona Khalil, who had refused to leave the beach she had spent years protecting, died from her injuries after the Israeli strike.

item.title: Bird survives arrow shot through neck, rescued by German firefighters
item.description: Firefighters in the west German city of Kleve close to the Dutch border have rescued a bird that had been shot with an arrow that lodged in its neck, the Kleve fire brigade said on Saturday. Despite the injury, the bird was still swimming briskly on a lake on Friday, the emergency services said.

item.title: Driver dies in England train crash, 9 still critical
item.description: A train driver is dead and nine people are still in critical condition after two passenger trains collided in England. About 80 people were injured.

item.title: Greece battles first major wildfire of the year
item.description: A major wildfire on the Greek island of Euboea - the country's first this year - prompted authorities to order precautionary evacuations on Saturday as strong winds drove the flames towards residential areas. The fire broke out near the villages of Mesochoria and Raptei in the afternoon, with the fire service urging residents to leave the area.

item.title: Al Jazeera cameraman Ahmed Wishah killed in Israeli attack in Gaza
item.description: Al Jazeera said it ‘strongly condemns the heinous crime of targeting and killing’ the Al Jazeera Mubasher correspondent.

item.description: The Iran war triggered airspace closures, grounding tens of thousands of flights and cutting off key global transit hubs.

item.title: Russian strike on Ukraine's Zaporizhzhya kills four
item.description: At least four people were killed and six injured in a Russian airstrike on the south-eastern Ukrainian city of Zaporizhzhya, regional authorities said on Saturday. Regional military governor Ivan Fedorov said on Telegram that nine aerial bombs struck the city centre.

item.title: Zelensky returns highest Polish honour as historical row deepens
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has returned Poland's highest state honour amid an escalating historical dispute between the two allies, after Polish President Karol Nawrocki stripped him of the award. Zelensky said on X he had sent back the Order of the White Eagle, which he received in 2023 as a symbol of friendship between Poland and Ukraine.

item.title: Russia frees 24 Filipinos after Philippine President Marcos talks with Putin
item.description: There were reports that the Filipinos may have been victims of illegal job recruitment, Philippine officials said.

item.title: At least one tourist dead after massive fire nearly destroys Caribbean resort, 1,690 evacuated
item.description: An Italian tourist died and nine others were injured after a massive fire swept through a Dominican Republic resort in Bayahibe, authorities say.

item.title: Iran says it's closing Strait of Hormuz and accuses US and Israel of violating ceasefire deal
item.description: Iran's military says it's blocking the vital waterway over Israeli strikes in Lebanon, but the US military says passage in the strait "remained intact".

item.title: As ILO convention turns 30, India’s home-based workers demand equal rights
item.description: Convention 177 was adopted on 20 June 1996, recognising home-based workers on a par with traditional wage earners.

item.title: Woman killed in stabbing rampage at Russian shopping centre
item.description: A woman was killed and several other people were injured in a stabbing rampage at a shopping centre in the southern Russian city of Krasnodar on Saturday, authorities said. The victim was attacked by a young man who randomly attacked people with a knife, police said.

item.title: Hundreds of swimmers lobby to end Berlin's ban on Spree River swimming
item.description: Several hundred people in Berlin took part in a swim-in protest on Saturday against the century-old ban on bathing in the city's Spree River. A spokesman for the Flussbad Berlin association said there were 500 swimmers and 500 people demonstrating on the banks.

item.title: Germany's The Left party elects co-leaders Pantisano and Schwerdtner
item.description: Germany's The Left Party on Saturday elected Luigi Pantisano alongside incumbent Ines Schwerdtner to lead the far-left party. At a party conference in Potsdam, Pantisano, a member of parliament, secured just over 53% despite running unopposed, a weak result.

item.title: Iran claims Strait of Hormuz closed in retaliation for US, Israel breaches of MoU
item.description: According to Mehr, Iran’s military declared the closure of the strait as the “first step” in its retaliation against what it deems violations of the US-Iran Memorandum of Understanding. Iran's top joint military command has ordered that the Strait of Hormuz be closed in retaliation for what it claims is a “blatant breach” of the recently signed US-Iran Memorandum of Understanding, according to Iranian semi-official state-sponsored media outlet Mehr on Saturday.

item.title: Bolivia declares state of emergency amid blockade crisis
item.description: For several weeks, a wave of protests has erupted over President Rodrigo Paz’s austerity measures.

item.title: Claude Guillemot, co-founder of Ubisoft, dies in plane crash
item.description: Claude Guillemot and a flight instructor were flying in a twin-motor Cessna 421 on Friday evening. An investigation into the crash is underway.

item.description: Iran said Israel's continued attacks in Lebanon are a breach of Tehran's agreement with the US to end the war.

item.title: The Strait of Hormuz is closing again. Few ships were leaving the waterway in the first place
item.description: An initial wave of ships exiting the Strait of Hormuz after it officially reopened on Thursday seems to have slowed down.

item.title: Russia frees 24 Filipinos after Marcos talks with Putin
item.description: Russia has freed 24 Filipinos who have been detained for months without charges in a Siberian city, after Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. raised concern for them in a meeting with President Vladimir Putin, Philippine officials said Saturday. The 24 were scheduled to arrive in Manila in two flights early Sunday, with the first batch to be welcomed by Philippine Foreign Secretary Theresa Lazaro, who accompanied Marcos in his talks with Putin on Wednesday in the Russian city of Kazan, the Department of Foreign Affairs in Manila said.

item.title: Iran says it's closing the Strait of Hormuz again, blaming Israeli strikes on Lebanon — just days after Trump's deal reopened it
item.description: Iran said Saturday that it is reclosing the Strait of Hormuz, citing what Tehran called the U.S. failure to stop Israel's strikes on Lebanon. It is the first crisis since President Trump's memorandum of understanding with Iran was signed earlier this week.

item.title: One killed as freight train plunges from bridge in Germany after crash
item.description: Clean-up efforts continued in Munich on Saturday after two freight trains collided on a railway bridge overnight, killing one person and sending two wagons crashing onto a road below. One of the two wagons that was dangling from the bridge was lifted onto the road below by a heavy-duty crane around midday, police said.

item.title: Italian tourist killed in Dominican Republic beach resort fire
item.description: A fire at the Viva Wyndham Dominicus Beach Hotel led to the evacuation of nearly 1,700 tourists. One woman died and several were treated for injuries.

item.title: Services returning to normal at Edinburgh Airport after security alert
item.description: Services at Edinburgh Airport are returning to normal on Saturday after it was evacuated on Friday evening because of a security alert. An explosive ordnance disposal team was deployed to the airport to investigate what Police Scotland described as a “potentially suspicious package”, which was discovered at about 6:50pm (1750 GMT) on Friday.

item.title: Germany calls for compliance with ceasefire under Iran deal
item.description: Germany on Saturday called on all parties to implement the ceasefire agreed as part of the framework deal between the United States and Iran. Berlin is using its contacts, particularly with Israeli partners, to help ensure that the ceasefire agreement is put into practice, a spokesman for the German Foreign Ministry said.

item.title: Norway crown princess' son sentenced to four years in prison on two counts of rape
item.description: Marius Borg Høiby, 29, is the eldest son of Crown Princess Mette-Marit from a previous relationship and the stepson of the heir to the throne, Crown Prince Haakon.

item.description: Iran said it has closed the Strait of Hormuz again as Israel and Lebanon continue fighting in spite of the peace plan between the U.S. and Iran.

item.title: Ukraine's Zelensky, top officials return Polish awards in WWII row
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky and his top officials said on Saturday they were returning Polish awards, in a spiralling row with key ally Poland over WWII massacres. Zelensky infuriated neighbouring Poland this month by naming a military unit after the nationalist Ukrainian Insurgent Army (UPA) that took part in massacres against Poles in WWII. On Friday, hard-right Polish President Karol Nawrocki stripped Zelensky of the Order of the White Eagle -- the highest honour in Poland -- despi

item.title: Russia reports drone attack on refinery in western Siberia
item.description: Drones have attacked an oil refinery in the western Siberian region of Tyumen, a senior Russian official said on Saturday. The facility was not damaged, according to the region's governor, Alexander Moor, who said workers were evacuated and emergency services were at the site of the resultant crash of debris.

item.description: Iran's military on Saturday said it will again close the Strait of Hormuz to all shipping, citing alleged Israeli violations of a ceasefire with Hezbollah in Lebanon that formed part of a recent framework deal between Tehran and Washington. In a statement reported in Iranian media, the military also pointed to Israel's continued presence in southern Lebanon and said shutting the key waterway for the global oil trade was "the first step" in responding to the "enemy's breach of trust.

item.title: Pakistan: US-Iran to begin technical talks in Switzerland on Sunday
item.description: Pakistan announced on Saturday that the technical-level talks between Iran and the United States to hammer out the complex operational details of their framework agreement will begin on Sunday. “As a follow-up to the signing of the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding, technical-level talks will be held in Bürgenstock, Switzerland," on Sunday, Pakistan’s foreign ministry said on Saturday.

item.title: French police detain 20 at banned Iran opposition rally
item.description: French police arrested around 20 people in Paris on Saturday as demonstrators gathered for a protest against repression and executions in Iran, defying an official ban.The demonstrators were eventually dispersed, and a score of people were arrested, a police source told AFP. "They arrested about 20 people for no reason," Afchine Alavi, a member of the National Council of Resistance of Iran (NCRI), one Iranian opposition group, told AFP. "On the protesters' side, there is no violence.

item.title: 'Windrush event helps teach about merged history'

item.title: Ubisoft Co-Founder Claude Guillemot Dead In Plane Crash In France
item.description: One of the founding members of Ubisoft, Claude Guillemot, died in a plane crash on Friday, June 19. He was 69.

item.title: Bolivia’s president declares a state of emergency as road blockades choke supplies
item.description: President Rodrigo Paz on Saturday declared a state of emergency that gives the military broad power to remove road blockades that have put a stranglehold on fuel and food supplies in Bolivia's seat of government and other major cities. A wave of protests over the last five weeks has called for Paz to step down over austerity measures imposed by the government, including the cancellation of fuel subsidies, and other issues.

item.title: Volcanic eruption injures sand miner in Indonesia
item.description: An eruption of Mount Semeru on Saturday left a sand miner with severe burns after he was struck by superheated volcanic material in Indonesia's East Java province, authorities and local media said. The eruption lasted about four minutes and generated pyroclastic flows, fast-moving avalanches of hot gas, ash and rock fragments from the volcano's summit, said Mukdas Sofian, a volcano observer at Indonesia's Centre for Volcanology and Geological Hazard Mitigation (PVMBG).

item.title: Hundreds turn out to counter Britain First demo
item.description: Police make arrests as Britain First and counter protesters gather in the city centre.

item.description: The number of cases has increased by 38 percent over the past week and has spread to 32 health zones across eastern Congo.

item.title: Hackers suspected to be behind unauthorized alert sent to cell phones across Brazil
item.description: An unauthorized alert bearing a mysterious message that was sent to cell phones in several states across Brazil on Saturday morning is suspected to be the work of hackers, the Brazilian government said.

item.description: An Italian woman was killed, nine injured and more than 1,700 evacuated from a massive fire at a beachfront resort in the Dominican Republic Friday.

item.title: A founder of Assassin's Creed maker Ubisoft killed in a plane crash in western France
item.description: A founder of global gaming company Ubisoft, maker of Assassin’s Creed, was killed in a plane crash in western France, authorities said Saturday. The twin-motor Cessna 421 carrying Claude Guillemot and a flight instructor crashed Friday evening near La Baule airport on the Atlantic coast, Mayor Franck Louvrier said in a statement. Ubisoft confirmed Guillemot's death but did not comment further.

item.title: Russia strikes an apartment building in the Ukrainian city of Kharkiv and 1 person is killed
item.description: Russian bombs struck an apartment building on Saturday in Kharkiv, Ukraine’s second-largest city, killing at least one person and wounding nine, including a 6-year-old child, authorities said. A body was pulled from the rubble hours after the attack, Mayor Ihor Terekhov said on Telegram. The head of the regional administration, Oleh Syniehubov, said that at least nine people were wounded, five of whom were hospitalized.

item.title: Spanish PM's wife must stand trial on corruption charges, judge rules
item.description: Source: Reuters

item.title: German MPs seek probe into development agency's Yemen operations
item.description: Lawmakers in Berlin are demanding an investigation into irregularities in the Yemen operations of the German government's GIZ development agency, which resulted in the dismissal of two dozen local staff members since 2023. "I take indications of a possible misappropriation of development aid funds very seriously," said Nicolas Zippelius, spokesman for the conservative CDU/CSU parliamentary group on development policy.

item.description: Poland has stripped Zelensky of their country's highest honor after he named a military unit after the Ukrainian Insurgent Army.

item.description: PARIS, June 20 (Reuters) - Protestors against political executions in Iran gathered in Paris on Saturday in defiance of a ban on the rally, at which police arrested 20 people, according to organisers.

item.title: Crowds gather in Coleraine to celebrate Armed Forces Day
item.description: Events included an Armed Forces Day parade, a ceremonial gun salute and an RAF Falcons parachute display.

item.title: UK train collision leaves 28 in hospital, nine in critical condition
item.description: Twenty eight people remain in hospital, nine of them in a critical condition, after two trains collided near Bedford, police have said. The crash, which killed a train driver, involved two East Midlands Railway (EMR) services, one smashing into the back of the other on the same line shortly after 5 pm (1600 GMT) on Friday.

item.title: UK condemns Israeli minister over inflammatory Lebanon remarks
item.description: UK foreign secretary slams Israeli minister’s inflammatory remarks amid regional tensions and fragile ceasefire.

item.title: Reports: Many killed in Israeli strikes on Lebanon despite ceasefire
item.description: Numerous Israeli airstrikes killed many people in Lebanon despite an agreed ceasefire, with the Lebanese civil defence authorities saying they had recovered 16 bodies in the southern city of Nabatiyeh alone, Lebanese media reported on Saturday. On Friday, US government sources said Israel and Lebanon's Iran-backed Hezbollah militia had agreed a new ceasefire after a major escalation in fighting between the two had threatened to derail a US-Iran deal to end the conflict in the region.

item.title: The Russian Navy has just shown its utter lack of seamanship
item.description: The Russian frigate Admiral Grigorovich firing warning shots across the bow of the British yacht Bright Future on June 16 was not an “isolated maritime misunderstanding” – no matter that our own Ministry of Defence would like you to think so. It illustrates two things: our own naval weakness, and the general incompetence of the Russian surface navy.

item.title: India's Cockroach Party supporters bang plates to call for education minister's resignation
item.description: Supporters of India’s viral Cockroach Janta Party banged steel plates with spoons in a protest Saturday to demand the resignation of the education minister over allegations of examination irregularities and repeated paper leaks. “There is a virus called Dharmendra Pradhan that has to be removed,” he told the crowd, adding that the CJP was open to talks with the government if Pradhan stepped down.

item.title: Israeli fire kills five people in Gaza, including a child, medics say
item.description: CAIRO, June 20 (Reuters) - Israeli strikes and gunfire killed at least five people, including a child, in the Gaza Strip on Saturday, health officials said.

item.title: UK police probe cause of train collision that killed driver and left 9 in critical condition
item.description: Nine people were in critical condition on Saturday after a collision between two passenger trains in central England the night before killed the driver of one of the locomotives, police said. British Transport Police Chief Constable Lucy D'Orsi said more than 80 people were treated in hospitals after the crash late Friday afternoon, and 28 remained hospitalized a day later. Buckingham Palace said King Charles III “is greatly saddened” by the crash.

item.description: Israel has stepped up attacks on parts of southern Lebanon, including Nabatieh, since midnight on Friday.

item.title: A freight trains' collision in Germany sends 2 wagons plunging off a bridge. One person was killed
item.description: Two freight trains collided on a railway bridge in Germany overnight, officials said Saturday, the crash sending two wagons plunging off the bridge and onto the street below. The wagons fell about 5 meters (16 feet) from the bridge, dpa said. Munich police said the street beneath the bridge has been closed and urged drivers to avoid the area while recovery and clearing operations were underway.

item.title: An Israeli strike hits a Gaza City apartment, killing 2 children, Palestinian health officials say
item.description: DEIR AL-BALAH, Gaza (AP) — An Israeli strike early Saturday killed at least two children in the Gaza Strip, Palestinian health officials said. Despite an October ceasefire between Israel and the militant group Hamas, the enclave has seen near-daily Israeli attacks that have killed over 1,007 Palestinians, the Gaza Health Ministry said. Saturday's strike hit an apartment in Gaza City around 2 a.m local time, according to the ministry, with the death toll expected to rise as rescue teams work on recovering more bodies.

item.description: Taiwan President Lai Ching-te said Taiwan’s efforts to strengthen its defenses should not be viewed as provocation, as Beijing intensifies military and political pressure and Taipei seeks swift approval of new U.S. arms sales. Taiwan Pushes Back On China On Thursday, speaking to the Taiwan Foreign Correspondents’ Club in Taipei, Lai said Taiwan’s actions are focused on protecting its democratic system and security, reported Reuters. item.description: A fire broke out at a resort in the Dominican Republic, claiming the life of one tourist and triggering a large-scale evacuation. Emergency responders moved quickly to clear the property, relocating roughly 1,690 guests to nearby hotels and other facilities. The incident has drawn renewed attention to safety preparedness at popular Caribbean destinations. Details of

item.title: China is counting its wins from the Iran war
item.description: When US and Israeli bombs first began falling on Iran at the end of February, China’s leaders were staring at the very real possibility of another friendly regime being decapitated, much like had happened with Venezuela only weeks before.

item.title: One dead and several wounded in Russian attack on Ukraine's Kharkiv
item.description: At least one person has been killed in a Russian air attack on the eastern Ukrainian city of Kharkiv, Governor Oleh Synyehubov said on Saturday. Rescue workers were recovering the person's body from the rubble after a glide bomb struck a two-storey apartment building, he posted on Telegram.

item.title: Report: Many killed in Israeli airstrikes in Lebanon despite ceasefire
item.description: Lebanese media on Saturday reported numerous Israeli airstrikes that killed many people despite an agreed ceasefire, with the Lebanese civil defence authorities saying they had recovered 16 bodies in the city of Nabatiyeh alone. On Friday, US government sources said Israel and Lebanon's Iranian-backed Hezbollah militia had agreed a new ceasefire after a major escalation in fighting between both had threatened to derail a US-Iran deal to end the conflict in the region.

item.title: Moscow oil refinery attack brings Russia's war with Ukraine closer to home
item.description: There are moments when life in Moscow feels completely normal - Thursday morning wasn't one of them, writes Steve Rosenberg.

item.title: Israel strikes south Lebanon despite truce announced with Hezbollah
item.description: Israel carried out deadly strikes in south Lebanon on Saturday and Hezbollah maintained it had the right to respond, hours after the United States announced a renewed ceasefire in fighting that had strained a fledgling deal with Iran.Hezbollah has not officially claimed any attacks on Israel or its troops in Lebanon since the ceasefire was announced.

item.title: 'Most wanted' suspect arrested in Belgium
item.description: Drugs and a firearm were recovered when Spencer Dillon Lamb was detained by Belgian police officers.

item.title: A top banker made a case for mining to Pope Leo XIV, who has seen its impact up close
item.description: The head of Latin America’s top development bank made a pitch to Pope Leo XIV this week in the face of the Vatican’s call to divest from the mining industry: that the mistakes of the past can be avoided in extracting rare earth minerals to supply a global tech boom. Ilan Goldfajn, head of the Inter-American Development Bank, met privately with the pope on Friday and asserted the potential of rare earth mining, saying it could be a boon to Latin America provided there are safeguards and value is added locally.

item.title: One killed as freight train plunges from bridge in Germany after crash
item.description: One person died after two freight trains collided on a bridge in Munich overnight, causing loaded cars to plunge to the road below, police said on Saturday. Two cars of one train fell about 5 metres from a bridge and came to rest at round 1:40 am (2340 GMT Friday), according to a spokeswoman.

item.title: Paradise inferno as fire engulfs Dominican resort, kills tourist
item.description: About 1,690 guests were evacuated and relocated to nearby hotels and other facilities, according to emergency services.

item.title: Ukrainian officials criticize Polish president's decision to strip Zelenskyy of honor
item.description: A decision by Polish President Karol Nawrocki to strip Ukrainian leader Volodymyr Zelenskyy of Poland’s highest state honor was beneficial to Moscow, which has an interest in seeing Kyiv in conflict with its allies, Ukrainian officials said. Nawrocki announced on Friday that he will strip Zelenskyy of the Order of the White Eagle over the Ukrainian leader’s decision to name a military unit after a Ukrainian paramilitary organization accused of massacring Poles during World War II.

item.title: Fighting persists in Lebanon despite a ceasefire as the US-Iran deal is under threat
item.description: The persistent fighting threatened an interim agreement between the United States and Iran to end the war in the Middle East. Lebanon’s National News Agency said the strikes hit the southern town of Nabatiyeh and nearby villages. Mediators were scrambling to halt the fighting between Israel and the militant Lebanese Hezbollah group, after a heavy exchange on Friday killed at least 47 people in Lebanon and four Israeli soldiers.

item.description: “What I'd dismissed as common was actually a fragile survivor of a vanishing written culture," the 28-year-old told Newsweek.

item.title: Pope Leo XIV heads to northern Italy to honor St. Augustine and migrant saint Cabrini
item.description: Pope Leo XIV is traveling Saturday to northern Italy to pay homage to two figures of Christian devotion: St. Augustine, the inspiration for his religious order, and Mother Frances Cabrini, the patron of migrants who became the first American saint. The pope's first stop Saturday is the Lombardy city of Pavia to pray at the tomb of St. Augustine, the fifth-century titan of early Christianity who centuries later inspired the formation of Leo's Augustinian religious order.

Nearly two weeks after his return to his homeland, James, 52, is trying to settle back into the life he left 10 years ago when he packed his suitcases and headed for South Africa on a six-month visitor’s visa. The attack on James came in the midst of a sharp rise in anti-migrant sentiment in South Africa in recent months, when there have been marches calling for immigrants in the country illegally to leave and reports of violence against some foreign nationals.

item.description: The war in Ukraine has now lasted longer than the First World War. As Britain struggles to build a modern fighting force against a backdrop of constrained resources and competing priorities, it is vital that we learn the right lessons from Ukraine. Equally importantly, however, we must not discard the hard-learnt lessons of the last century, from the Somme and Passchendaele to the Donbas front line.

item.title: Photos show Lebanon's shattered south as towns and villages await peace
item.description: On Friday, officials said Israel and Hezbollah agreed to stop attacking each other, raising fresh hopes that the conflict could ease after repeated ceasefires failed to hold. In the coastal city of Tyre, after a U.S.-Iran preliminary deal to end the war in the region was signed, residents returned to neighborhoods reduced to rubble by Israeli strikes. Apartment buildings stand gutted, streets are lined with debris, and families are beginning to assess the damage left behind.

BTP said their officers responded to reports of the crash at around 5:15 pm (1615 GMT) on Friday.

item.title: Tyre in southern Lebanon marks Muharram holy month after destruction from Israel-Hezbollah war
item.description: Wearing a yellow scarf showing her son killed fighting for Hezbollah in southern Lebanon, Iman Dilbani wept during a religious sermon Friday in the battered southern city of Tyre during the Islamic holy month of Muharram. Tyre, Lebanon’s fourth largest city, has been devastated by the Israel-Hezbollah war that reached a reported ceasefire Friday, with damaged buildings and structures reduced to rubble seen on almost every street following intense Israeli airstrikes. Muharram is among the holiest months for Shiite Muslims and marks the martyrdom of the Prophet Muhammad’s grandson, Imam Hussein, and his 72 companions in the battle of Karbala in the seventh century in present-day Iraq.

item.description: African officials must step up financing to respond and develop vaccines for the Ebola outbreak in Congo and Uganda, the continent's top health agency said on Friday, warning that the continent cannot continue to rely on foreign partners for its health needs. The outbreak has claimed more than 200 lives out of 894 confirmed cases since May 15, with up to 35,000 contacts, the Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said.

item.title: Two trains collide north of London, killing at least one person
item.description: Emergency services rushed to the scene of a collision between two trains north of London on Friday afternoon that killed at least one person. A passenger reported that he was thrown forward by the impact then saw fellow travelers with broken bones and bloody injuries. Both trains were traveling south to London St. Pancras station when they collided outside the town of Bedford around 5:15 p.m., according to information on rail tracking websites.

item.title: Ghana conference urges slave-trade nations to issue apologies and reparations
item.description: African and Caribbean leaders in Ghana on Friday urged former slave-trading nations to issue apologies and reparations over the trafficking of enslaved Africans after a landmark U.N. resolution in March declaring it “the gravest crime against humanity.” The “Next Steps” conference in the Ghanaian capital of Accra issued a declaration calling on countries involved in the Atlantic slave trade to “offer full, formal and unconditional apologies as a foundational step towards reconciliation, trust-building and reparatory justice.”
