item.title: Kim Gamel, a former veteran AP correspondent in Europe and the Middle East, dies
item.description: Kim Gamel, a former veteran Associated Press correspondent who covered conflicts and uprisings around the Middle East, including the height of the Iraq war and the Arab Spring, has died, according to her brother-in-law. Gamel died Wednesday in Idaho after a yearslong battle with lymphoma, Lee Ruff said. Gamel brought the same care and compassion to her work reporting and directing coverage in war zones that she offered to her colleagues.

item.title: Mourners bury a 6-month-old Ebola victim, marking third orphanage death as Congo outbreak spreads
item.description: Mourners gathered Friday to bury a 6-month-old girl who died from Ebola earlier this week, the third child to die at an orphanage in eastern Congo as authorities have struggled to contain the latest outbreak. Carrying a cross, people stood at a distance as the small coffin was lowered into the ground by masked and gloved health workers, and a Catholic priest prayed over her body. Ituri, the region at the center of the current outbreak, has reported more than 90% of the cases.

item.title: Police probe attack on German Interior Ministry official's car

On Thursday morning, a car owner noticed a strong, foul odour coming from his car at his home address and reported it to the police later that day, a police spokeswoman said, without mentioning de Vries’ name.

Ten migrants survived the shipwreck, which occurred on June 12 in the Mediterranean Sea, off the eastern part of the North African nation, according to the Abreen group, which tracks movements of migrants in eastern Libya. It was the latest tragedy in this part of the Mediterranean Sea.

Nancy Napoles is the municipal president of the Mexican town of Tenancingo and is accused of carrying out an elaborate plot to collect $2 million in embezzled public funds disguised as a ransom. Prosecutors said she was forced out of a car at gunpoint, with three people now arrested and her husband and brother-in-law on the run. No warrant had been issued for her arrest at the time of writing.

item.title: German journalist detained in Syria has been released, family confirms
item.description: A German journalist arrested in Syria in mid-January, Eva Maria Michelmann, has been released, her family confirmed on Friday. Her brother, Antonius Michelmann, told dpa she had been released, confirming reports published by the news magazine Der Spiegel.

item.title: Snake catcher pulls 7.5-foot black mamba from beneath bed after home search
item.description: They are fast, highly venomous, and capable of causing life-threatening bites.

item.title: Pétanque player, 68, dies after being 'hit in head with metal boule'
item.description: The man was allegedly struck by his 81-year-old opponent, who has since been arrested, the public prosecutor says.

item.description: Hebridean Island Cruises said crew responded immediately to Tuesday's incident.

item.title: German journalist detained in Syria has been released
item.description: A German journalist arrested in Syria in mid-January, Eva Maria Michelmann, has been released, her family confirmed on Friday. Her brother, Antonius Michelmann, told dpa she had been released, confirming reports published by the news magazine Der Spiegel.

item.title: Hormuz ship traffic climbs after war deal: trackers
item.description: Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz rose to its busiest in two months after a deal to halt the US-Iran war, maritime trackers said on Friday.The spike came after Iran and the United States agreed this week to re-open the crucial route under an agreement to end the war, but before the postponement of talks between the sides in Switzerland that had been planned for Friday under that deal.

item.title: Funny old world: the week's offbeat news
item.description: From Germany's most surprising supporter to the duck who has become Mexico's World Cup superstar.For the 2006 World Cup -- which Germany hosted -- he started out small, commissioning a 1.5-kilometre-long flag.

item.title: Resident locks cat in chicken coop to save it from stray dogs, then finds python too full to move
item.description: The snake did not attack the chickens, apparently.

item.title: ICC's Karim Khan suspended from British bar for sexual allegations
item.description: Karim Khan, chief prosecutor for the International Criminal Court, has been suspended by Britain's Bar Standards Board for alleged sexual misconduct.

item.title: Armenia's opposition faces arrests as it contests the ruling party's election win
item.description: Several members of Armenia's opposition were arrested Friday while a major pro-Russia party asked the top court to annul this month’s election win by Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan’s ruling party, alleging electoral violations and suppression of the opposition. The opposition Strong Armenia party appealed to the Constitutional Court to invalidate election results or call a second round of voting, and denounced the arrests of opposition politicians as an assault on democracy.

item.title: ‘Wake up’: US forces Israel into Hezbollah ceasefire after stark warning
item.description: Is Donald Trump’s US-Iran peace deal already falling apart?

item.title: 10,000 infected in major cholera outbreak in northern Nigeria
item.description: More than 10,000 people have been infected with Cholera in a major outbreak centred on Nigeria’s conflict-hit northeastern Borno state.

item.title: Minimum age of 11 set for UK puberty blocker trial
item.description: Gender-questioning children will have to be at least 11 years old to take part in a clinical trial of puberty-blocking drugs.

item.title: Israel continues attacks on Lebanon despite agreeing to ceasefire
item.description: At least 47 people killed in Israeli attacks on Lebanon since midnight as US-Iran talks stall.

item.title: How Ukraine broke through Russia’s air defenses
item.description: As a barrage of Ukrainian drones descended on Moscow in the early hours of Thursday, Russia’s response appeared more like a scramble, and less like a well-planned strategic defense. Videos from the streets of the capital showed glimpses of the ensuing chaos.

item.title: Fatalities caused by Russian shelling in Kramatorsk
item.description: At least two people have been killed by Russian shelling in the eastern Ukrainian city of Kramatorsk. Six other people were injured, according to Vadym Filashkin, the military governor of the Donetsk region, who posted the news on Telegram on Friday.

item.title: French mountain lodges worry over strained water supply
item.description: No spring, no river, no lake: "Every litre counts" at French mountain lodges where water is scarce but visitor numbers are booming."Every litre counts, of course.

item.title: Canadian auto parts magnate Frank Stronach found guilty of sexual assault
item.description: Austrian-Canadian billionaire and automotive business founder Frank Stronach was found guilty Friday of sexual assault and indecent assault of two woman decades ago. Stronach, who is 93, had been accused of alleged incidents involving seven complainants, and pleaded not guilty to 12 charges. Ontario Superior Court Justice Anne Molloy found Stronach guilty on Friday on two charges related to two complainants.

item.title: Germany's IKEA stores affected as workers go on strike
item.description: Thousands of retail workers across Germany have once again walked out in the current round of collective bargaining. The focus of Friday's action, called by the Verdi trade union, was the furniture retailer IKEA.

item.title: Brazen attack on Niger’s airport shows jihadis are expanding to cities in Africa's Sahel
item.description: The gunfire and explosions that ripped through Niger’s main international airport are the latest sign of armed groups increasingly targeting cities and urban centers in Africa’s Sahel region, where they are competing for influence and territories, analysts say. The al-Qaeda-linked JNIM jihadi group, the most potent in the Sahel region south of the Sahara desert, claimed responsibility for Thursday's attack at Diori Hamani International Airport in Niamey that killed 11 soldiers and two civilians. It's also the headquarters of the regional alliance that bring together troops from Niger, Mali and Burkina Faso.

item.title: Hundreds of nurses given safety alarms after racist violence
item.description: The alarms were distributed to international nurses based at Altnagelvin Hospital in Londonderry.

item.title: Israel, Hezbollah agree truce, US says, as fighting derails Iran talks
item.description: Israel and the Iranian-backed Hezbollah militia in Lebanon have agreed a new ceasefire, US government sources said on Friday, after a major escalation in fighting threatened to derail diplomatic efforts to end the conflict in the region. The agreement, brokered by the United States and Qatar, came into effect at 1300 GMT, the sources said.

item.title: ‘Hideous’: Left and Right Slam Israeli Minister Declaring ‘All of Lebanon Must Burn’
item.description: Jake Tapper replied, “Ben Gvir, an anti-Arab racist zealot, says ‘all of Lebanon must burn.’ That’s almost 6 million people. Hideous.”

item.title: Photos show Nagas protesting against rising cases of sexual violence
item.description: Thousand of people from the Naga community marched in pouring rain on Friday to demand justice for victims of sexual violence after a spate of cases reported in the state. In Kohima, the capital of Nagaland, the protesters said it was time to break the silence around sexual violence for fear of societal recrimination and discrimination. The small mountain state, that has consistently ranked as one of the safest places for women in India, has been shaken by a chain of sexual assaults in the past few months.

item.title: Fisherman Missing at Sea for 7 Days Found Alive Wearing T-Shirt and Shorts in Tiny 13-Foot Boat

item.description: The development comes after the U.S.-Iran peace talks set to take place in Switzerland on Friday were abruptly postponed.

item.title: Norway imposes near ban on AI in elementary school
item.description: Norway is imposing a near ban on the use of generative AI tools by elementary school pupils while ‌also restricting their use in the education of older children to ‌prevent a negative impact on learning, the country's prime minister said on Friday. Facing a broad ​decline in education test scores, the government in 2024 banned smartphones from schools and has given teachers back more powers to enforce discipline in the classroom. Using AI increases the risk that young children skip important steps in their education, ‌Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Stoere ⁠told a press conference on Friday.

item.description: Icelandic whalers are resuming their controversial hunt for fin whales after a two-year pause, animal welfare campaigners said on Friday. Two Icelandic whaling vessels left the port in the capital, Reykjavík, Pro Wildlife and other animal welfare organizations said.

item.title: Zelensky: Ukraine effectively has 'NATO's second-strongest army'
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has described his country's armed forces as effectively "NATO's second-strongest army. " Zelensky told journalists that "NATO needs us," the Hromadske website reported on Friday.

item.description: A recent vascular care reform whitepaper for England highlighted gaps in care and issues that cause avoidable harm to patients.

item.description: Turkey's interior ministry has instructed governors nationwide not to permit the installation of giant public screens for the national team's World Cup match against Paraguay, citing the need to minimise noise and traffic disruption during the country's university entrance examinations. The YKS is Turkey's nationwide university entrance examination and is taken annually by hundreds of thousands of students seeking admission to higher education institutions.

item.description: The ceasefire renewal comes after 18 people were killed in Lebanon following overnight Israeli air strikes, despite the U.S.-Iran MOU.

Matt Twist, the Metropolitan Police’s assistant commissioner, says just a few repeat thieves are responsible for thousands of crimes.

item.description: Iranian officials and state media have celebrated it as a triumph for the Islamic Republic and a defeat for the United States. But not everyone in Iran sees it that way, and many of the deal’s critics are both influential and well-connected.

item.description: At least 10 people have died in a refugee camp near the centre of the Congo’s Ebola epidemic, raising fears of a new disease outbreak.

item.title: Ebola spreading 'fast' in DR Congo, warns WHO
item.description: The fatal Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo is spreading rapidly, the World Health Organization warned Friday, despite accelerating efforts to tackle the virus.The outbreak has also spread to North Kivu and South Kivu provinces.

item.title: Trapped on Everest for days, Nepali survivor recounts escape
item.description: For three days, Nepali mountaineer Dawa Sherpa was trapped deep inside an Everest crevasse, surviving on biscuits, chocolates and chunks of ice, while back home his family had already begun mourning his death.But he was still deep inside the crevasse.

item.description: The Nao Santa Maria arrives at Sovereign Harbour where the public can come on board.

item.title: Spanish king and crown princess on joint training flight
item.description: Spain’s Crown Princess Leonor, 20, and her father, King Felipe VI, 58, have completed their first joint training flight in Spanish Air Force aircraft. Each flew a two-seater Pilatus PC-21 turboprop aircraft from the airbase of the Spanish Air Force Officers’ Academy over the Mediterranean, according to a statement published online by the Royal Household on Friday.

item.title: ICC prosecutor temporary suspended from British bar
item.description: The British bar association temporarily suspended the embattled chief prosecutor of the International Criminal Court on Friday, pending the outcome of disciplinary proceedings. British barrister Karim Khan was formally removed from his duties at the ICC last week after the court's oversight body found he had engaged in “serious misconduct” over accusations he had an inappropriate relationship with a female staff member. The move by the Bar Standards Board, which regulates lawyers practicing in England and Wales, is the latest challenge Khan is facing.

item.title: France's Thales teams up with Hanwha on long-range strike systems
item.description: French technology group Thales and South Korea's Hanwha Aerospace have agreed to cooperate on land-based long-range precision strike systems. The French firm announced on Friday that the two companies have signed a memorandum of understanding to this effect.

item.title: Stepmum jailed for 1978 killing of girl, 5
item.description: Andrea suffered severe burns to half her body and died in hospital on 13 July 1978.

item.title: A conflict over cattle in Brazil's Amazon highlights tensions for Indigenous peoples
item.description: On a vast island in northern Brazil, an unusual debate about cattle and conservation is taking place. Federal authorities last year ordered the removal of herds from protected Indigenous territory on the world's largest river island, Bananal Island. The events underscore the challenge of balancing conservation, Indigenous interests and pressure from agribusiness, one of Brazil’s most powerful sectors.

item.title: G-7 Vows Crackdown on North Korean Crypto Theft Funding Nuclear Arms
item.description: Group of Seven leaders pledged a coordinated crackdown on North Korea’s multi-billion dollar cryptocurrency t...

item.title: Russia attacks merchant ships in Black Sea, killing crew member
item.description: The Russian military has attacked two civilian merchant ships in the Black Sea with drones, Ukrainian authorities reported on Friday. Deputy Prime Minister Oleksii Kuleba and the port administration said a crew member on a Panamanian-flagged vessel was killed in the attack, while two other seafarers were injured, one of them seriously.

item.title: Fresh Israeli strikes trigger new exodus in southern Lebanon
item.description: Renewed Israeli airstrikes on southern Lebanon have prompted hundreds of residents to flee their homes once again, underscoring the fragility of a US-Iran framework agreement that was intended to end regional violence. Scores of cars packed with families were seen heading north from southern Lebanon toward the port city of Sidon on Friday, seeking shelter from the escalating fighting.

item.title: Israel strikes southern Lebanon as fighting strains peace efforts
item.description: The Israeli military launched fresh airstrikes on southern Lebanon on Friday, as renewed fighting with the Hezbollah militia threatened to derail diplomatic efforts to end the Iran war. Airstrikes were carried out overnight and on Friday morning on positions of the Shiite Hezbollah militia in southern Lebanon, the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) said.

item.title: Seven men plead not guilty to child rape
item.description: They deny 38 offences, including rape and sex trafficking.

item.title: IDF strikes 80 Hezbollah targets, kills dozens of terrorists after four soldiers killed
item.description: After four Israeli soldiers were killed, the IDF also announced that they had attacked two Hezbollah command centers in the Beqaa Valley, while terrorists were inside. The IDF struck over 80 Hezbollah targets across Lebanon overnight in response to repeated violations of the ceasefire by the terrorist group, the military announced on Friday.

item.title: Hundreds of Thousands of Mice Are Swarming Australia’s Farms
item.description: A mouse plague of Biblical proportions has struck parts of Australia, and it’s not for the first time. The startling scenes in this Instagram post show hundreds of thousands of mice streaming around agricultural equipment and across roads. Farmers and the authorities can only do their best against a relentless onslaught. Australia’s Worst Mouse Plague

item.title: WHO reports 75 health-care workers infected with Ebola
item.description: The World Health Organization (WHO) has reported that 75 health-care workers have been infected with the dangerous Ebola virus during the outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda. Seventeen of them have died, Marie-Roseline Belizaire, the WHO's representative in Bunia, told reporters in Geneva on Friday.

item.title: EU leaders squabble over outreach to Moscow as Ukraine war rages on
item.description: European Union leaders have been unable to agree on setting up a back-channel with Moscow to ensure that the bloc’s interests are protected should progress be made in negotiations to end Russia’s war on Ukraine, some of them said on Friday. European Council President António Costa, who chaired their two-day summit, had directed his office to reach out to the Kremlin and proposed a senior official to make contact. Costa said his aim was not to mediate or set up a parallel negotiating track to the one led by the United States, which is making little progress.

item.title: Iranian Singer Parastoo Ahmadi Reportedly Sentenced to 74 Lashes Over Hijab-Free YouTube Concert

item.description: Kenya has large, untapped deposits of minerals required to build infrastructure for renewable energy and digital infrastructure.

item.title: New Russian strikes hit Kharkiv and Odesa, killing one and injuring children
item.description: New Russian strikes hit Ukraine's Kharkiv and Odesa regions on 19 June, killing one person, injuring children and adults, and damaging homes, vehicles and fuel facilities overnight.

item.title: Afghanistan launches 'airstrikes' on Pakistan
item.description: Afghanistan says it has attacked several targets inside Pakistan by air in the latest flare-up in the simmering conflict between the neighbouring countries. The Afghan Ministry of Defence wrote on X on Friday that it had carried out "airstrikes" at various locations inside Pakistan.

item.title: Armenia sees arrests and calls for election rerun
item.description: Pro-Russian politicians in Armenia have called for a rerun of the country's parliamentary election following the arrest of several opposition figures in the South Caucasus republic. The Strong Armenia bloc, led by billionaire Samvel Karapetyan, has filed a petition with the Constitutional Court seeking the annulment of the election results, local media reported on Friday.

item.title: 2 men sentenced to prison for arson on property linked to UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer
item.description: A British court sent two men to prison Friday for setting fire to property linked to British Prime Minister Keir Starmer in a plot orchestrated by a mysterious Russian-speaking figure. Ukrainian national Roman Lavrynovych, 22, and Stanislav Carpiuc, a 27-year-old Romanian citizen, were imprisoned for 7 years and 2 years, respectively, after being found guilty of conspiracy to damage property by fire. The men targeted a car and two properties linked to Starmer over three nights in May 2025 on the orders of a Russian-speaking figure going by the name of “El Money,” according to prosecutors.

item.title: Afghanistan strikes targets in Pakistan, raising cross-border tension
item.description: The strikes are the latest threat to the fragile ceasefire between Afghanistan and Pakistan.

item.title: Ukraine's small drone teams keep beating NATO units in exercises, and neither side is surprised
item.description: Ukraine has more battlefield drone experience than many NATO allies, and it's eager to share its hard-won expertise.

item.title: Brezhnev’s great-grandson ‘captured by Ukraine’
item.description: The great-grandson of Leonid Brezhnev, the Soviet leader, was captured while fighting in Ukraine, Russian media has claimed.

item.title: More than 70 Congo medics infected with Ebola since outbreak started, WHO says
item.description: NAIROBI, June 19 (Reuters) - A senior World Health Organization official said on Friday that 75 ‌medics in the Democratic Republic of Congo had ‌been infected with Ebola and 17 of them had died since the ​current outbreak started there. Ebola was thought to be circulating months before the outbreak was first declared by Congolese officials on May 15, meaning many medics were exposed to the disease ‌before they even ⁠knew it was present.

item.description: The interceptor drone systems are expected to be installed by 2027 near radar sites, bases, vessels and other critical locations, the agency stated.

item.title: UNICEF: Ceasefire in Gaza a 'deadly illusion' for children
item.description: The ceasefire in the Gaza Strip announced in October is “a deadly illusion” for children and young people, the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) said on Friday. UNICEF spokesman James Elder reported that 265 minors have been killed since then, more than 90% of them as a result of Israeli attacks.

item.title: Survey: Majority in favour of German Strait of Hormuz deployment
item.description: Most people in Germany would welcome the country's armed forces taking part in an operation to secure the Strait of Hormuz, if the ceasefire in Iran holds. A survey conducted for public broadcaster ZDF found that 57% would welcome Germany joining other European countries in such an operation, while 38% were not in favour.

item.description: The ceasefire declared in Gaza more than eight months ago is a "deadly illusion", the UN charged on Friday, with 265 children killed there since the fighting was supposed to have stopped.Yet for Palestinian children, this so-called ceasefire has become a cruel and a deadly illusion."

item.title: Netanyahu: IDF will exact 'very heavy price' from Hezbollah after deaths of four soldiers
item.description: "I told the Prime Minister, even in our private meetings: For every tear of an Israeli mother, a thousand Lebanese mothers must weep," Ben-Gvir wrote on X/Twitter. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu instructed the IDF to strike at Hezbollah "with full force" in response to four IDF soldiers being killed by Hezbollah on Friday.

item.title: Former member of Regensburg boys' choir loses abuse damages case
item.description: A former member of Germany's renowned Regensburger Domspatzen (Cathedral Sparrows) boys' choir has lost a lawsuit seeking damages from the Catholic diocese of Regensburg over alleged abuse he says he suffered as a child. The Regensburg Regional Court dismissed the claim on the grounds that it was time-barred under Germany's statute of limitations, a court spokesman said on Friday.

item.title: Elder fraud cases jump 61% in Israel as abuse reports rise, Welfare Ministry says
item.description: Of the cases where an elder was defrauded, 92% of the perpetrators were family members of the victim, with 48% their children and 33% their spouse. Elder fraud cases jumped by 61% in Israel over 2025, the Welfare Ministry announced on Monday in a report for World Elder Abuse Awareness Day.

item.title: Russia sentences Romanian man to 15 years for spying for Ukraine
item.description: Russia's FSB domestic intelligence service said on Friday that a Romanian citizen arrested on charges of spying for Ukraine in Russia had been sentenced to 15 years in a penal colony. The man, born in 2002, had been surveying Russian air-defence sites in the Black Sea city of Sochi on behalf of Ukraine, the FSB said in a press release.

item.title: Over 50 detained in Turkey raids targeting opposition offices
item.description: Turkish authorities expanded raids targeting municipalities run by the main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP), detaining dozens of suspects in corruption probes in Istanbul and the southern province of Mersin, state broadcaster TRT reported on Friday. In an Istanbul-based probe, covering four cities, prosecutors ordered the detention of 47 suspects over alleged bribery, corruption and organized crime.

item.title: Clip of Iranian soldiers firing at US military helicopter is AI-generated
item.description: Iran and the United States engaged in tit-for-tat strikes in June 2026 before they reached a deal aimed at ending the Middle East war, but a video of Iranian troops shooting down a US helicopter circulating online does not show the attack. The clip shows visual errors and was flagged by an AI detection tool that it was fabricated.

item.title: Man arrested after 3-year-old injured in zoo's crocodile enclosure
item.description: British authorities have arrested a 30-year-old man on suspicion of attempted murder after a 3-year-old child ended up in a zoo's crocodile enclosure.

item.title: Israel continues strikes on Lebanon despite US-Iran agreement
item.description: Airstrikes were carried out overnight and on Friday morning on positions of the Shiite Hezbollah militia in southern Lebanon, the Israeli military has said. The military stated on X that the strikes followed Hezbollah rocket fire targeting Israeli soldiers.

item.title: Deadly Philippines quake turns seabed into shore
item.description: Arsenio Butil Jr. fell to his knees and began to pray when last week's deadly 7.8-magnitude earthquake began shaking his home on the coast of the southern Philippines.In Glan, residents were thinking about next steps, but not yet ready to attempt to rebuild their destroyed homes, said Butil Jr, the pastor.

item.title: Zimbabwe MPs pass bill to extend president's time in power
item.description: The proposal would extend Emmerson Mnangagwa's term by two years and scrap direct presidential elections.

item.title: Haifa court stops auction of yellow star, Holocaust-era documents
item.description: Yad Ezer La-Haver operates the Holocaust Museum in Haifa and manages the shelter that provides lodging and food for tens of Holocaust survivors. The Magistrates Court in Haifa issued an immediate restraining order, forbidding the auctioning of artifacts from the Holocaust, Walla reported on Wednesday.

item.title: Freed Israeli hostage Guy Gilboa-Dalal recounts sexual abuse in Gaza, urges victims to speak out
item.description: An Israeli man who said he was sexually abused while he was held hostage in the Gaza Strip is hoping to use his voice to help empower victims who have suffered similar assaults, including in conflict zones, he said in remarks ahead of the International Day for the Elimination of Sexual Violence in Conflict. Guy Gilboa-Dalal, 25, spent two years in captivity in Gaza after Palestinian militants abducted him and 250 others during the Hamas-led attacks in Israel on Oct. 7, 2023. “I feel like I have a mission to spread to the world, to use my voice and empower other victims of sexual assaults,” he said Sunday in a conversation with Israel's first lady Michal Herzog in Jerusalem.

item.title: Israel strikes Lebanon risking Trump's Iran peace deal
item.description: Israel's military said it carried out overnight strikes that continued into Friday morning targeting Hezbollah infrastructure.

item.title: Exclusive-Tata's iPhone parts factory faces India health probe after farmer contamination complaints
item.description: By Munsif Vengattil and Aditya Kalra HOSUR, India June 19 (Reuters) - An Indian state health authority is investigating how liquid discharged from Apple supplier Tata's iPhone components factory has

item.title: Old Karachi protest footage misrepresented as clashes in Pakistan-administered Kashmir
item.description: Clashes between police and members of a banned protest movement in June 2026 killed at least 22 people in Pakistan-administered Kashmir, but a clip circulating online showing officers firing on demonstrators was not filmed during the recent protests. Visual evidence indicates it was in fact recorded months ago in Karachi at protests sparked by the killing of Iran's supreme leader.

item.title: Rheinmetall boss urges rules on AI use in weapons
item.description: Rheinmetall chief executive Armin Papperger has called for global rules to ensure that humans, rather than machines using artificial intelligence (AI), continue to decide when weapons are fired in war. Papperger told dpa at the Eurosatory defence exhibition in Villepinte near Paris that technological possibilities in the defence industry were likely to expand exponentially over the next 10 years.

item.title: Several injured in Russian airstrike on Ukraine's Kharkiv
item.description: At least nine people, including four children, were injured in a heavy Russian airstrike on the Ukrainian city of Kharkiv, officials said on Friday. More than 40 private homes were damaged when guided glide bombs were dropped on the city, Kharkiv Mayor Ihor Terekhov said on Telegram.

item.title: Switzerland says planned US-Iran talks called off
item.description: A planned first round of talks between the United States and Iran on the details of a framework agreement has been called off, the Swiss foreign ministry said. "The talks at the Bürgenstock will not take place today as planned," the ministry in Bern said on Friday, without giving a reason.

item.title: Police charge a third suspect in a Melbourne synagogue arson allegedly directed by Iran
item.description: Police charged a third suspect on Friday with an arson attack on a Melbourne synagogue that was allegedly directed by Iran. The 20-year-old man was one of three masked offenders who broke into the Adass Israel Synagogue, doused the interior with flammable liquid then set it alight in the early hours of Dec. 6, 2024, a police statement alleged. The fire caused extensive damage to the synagogue and a worshipper sustained minor injuries.

item.title: No vaccine, conflict, mistrust: Ebola's return to DR Congo
item.description: Ebola is spreading again in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, where conflict, mistrust and the lack of a vaccine have made the outbreak hard to contain. - The DRC's 17th Ebola outbreak was officially declared on May 15 but is believed to have been spreading under the radar for weeks.

item.description: "I knew there was a chance that the cougar would turn on me so I’d better make whatever I did count."

item.title: Arnold Whittall, musicologist and leading Wagner scholar
item.description: Arnold Whittall, the musicologist who has died aged 90, did not claim to know everything in his field, merely to have read everything; he was a leading figure in the study of 20th- and 21st-century music, his sharp observations and provocative challenges helping students towards a greater understanding and appreciation of the complexities of music from that time.

item.description: Every two minutes, a woman dies from complications related to pregnancy or childbirth.

item.description: After the shock of being diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease a year ago, Pearl Bartlett has found herself facing an unexpected additional challenge: the monthly ordeal of simply locating the medication she needs to manage daily life.

item.description: When Emmerson Mnangagwa came to power in the 2017 coup that toppled Robert Mugabe, celebrations on the streets of Harare were matched by relief in chancelleries from London to Beijing.

item.title: Israeli military strikes in southern Lebanon in intense fighting as US-Iran talks postponed
item.description: Israel’s military said Friday its forces struck targets throughout southern Lebanon overnight as Hezbollah reported intense fighting in the area. Lebanon’s state-run National News Agency reported at least 16 people were killed in Israeli airstrikes. The attacks came as planned talks in Switzerland between Iran and the United States over their efforts to reach a permanent end to the Iran war were delayed.

item.title: India court rejects Telegram's appeal against temporary blocking of app
item.description: A New Delhi court on Friday rejected Telegram's appeal against a temporary ban in India, a ‌setback for the messaging app which had challenged the prohibition imposed to ‌curb alleged exam-related fraud. India will conduct its national undergraduate medical entrance exam on Sunday, a month ​after it cancelled the results of the same test after allegations that the questions were leaked in advance. The unprecedented ban on Telegram until June 22 was announced this week by India's IT ministry over concerns about channels on the ‌app claiming to have questions ⁠from the upcoming exam for sale.

item.title: Fact Check: Image Was Altered With OpenAI Tools To Make German Soccer Fan Look Like Adolf Hitler
item.description: Does footage of the Germany vs. Curaçao World Cup Game show a fan in the stands who looked just like Adolf Hitler and was rooting for Germany? No, that's not true: The screenshot of the FIFA World Cup match footage...

item.description: The artwork, which once hung in Oxburgh Hall, depicts a scene from the Old Testament Book of Esther.

item.title: About 300 children and teachers evacuated or rescued after fire breaks out at a Tokyo school
item.description: A fire broke out at an elementary school in downtown Tokyo Friday, but all of about 300 students and teachers were evacuated or rescued, officials said. The Tokyo Fire Department said the fire at the Takinogawa No. 3 Elementary School broke out near a music room on the top floor of the four-story building late morning Friday. Television footage showed black smoke billowing out of windows on the fourth floor, as firefighters combated the fire at the scene.

item.title: Boston To Formally Name Glasgow, Scotland Twin City
item.description: Boston has cemented its ties to Scotland permanently.

item.title: Cambodian Supreme Court upholds incitement conviction of opposition politician in charged case
item.description: Cambodia's Supreme Court on Friday upheld the incitement conviction of a prominent opposition politician while suspending the remainder of his sentence, keeping him out of prison but unable to practice politics for years. The decision against Rong Chhun, a top adviser to the Nation Power Party, was met with anger by supporters gathered outside the Supreme Court complex in Phnom Penh. Under the decision, Rong Chhun will be prohibited from any involvement in politics for five years, including voting or standing as a candidate, and will be banned from traveling abroad for three years, the remainder of his original four-year sentence, his attorney Em Chantha told reporters.

item.title: More Than 400 Cats Rescued in Major Vietnam Cat Meat Trade Bust, Dozens Reunited with Owners

item.title: New Zealand minister defends fishers after two orcas killed in net
item.description: A New Zealand minister has defended fishers involved in the deaths of two orcas in commercial fishing incidents and said "these things happen", while conservationists condemned the "devastating" loss.Fishing minister Shane Jones described the deaths of the critically endangered mammals in separate incidents as an "unpleasant outcome", adding he "thought orcas were quite intelligent".

item.title: Baseless claim about changes to Sri Lankan law kindles ethnic, religious tensions
item.description: Government officials have refuted claims Sri Lanka will incorporate Sharia law into national legislation, contradicting posts that say the move was announced by the country's Muslim deputy minister for religious and cultural affairs. While the Buddhist-majority country has Sinhalese, Tamil and Muslim laws, they are limited and only apply to those specific communities. A mocked-up image of a newspaper's front page, included in the posts in an apparent attempt to legitimise the claim, also contain

item.title: About 100 Colombian guerrilla dissidents disarm under peace talks with government
item.description: About a hundred Colombian guerrilla dissidents on Thursday surrendered their weapons in a step toward their gradual reintegration into civilian life as part of a peace process with the government of President Gustavo Petro. Dressed in military-style camouflage, the members of the National Coordinating Committee of the Bolivarian Army placed their weapons on a table during a formal ceremony in the department of Putumayo, which borders Ecuador. The group is a dissident faction of the defunct Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia, or FARC.

item.title: Australia is ditching 10,000 miles of power lines as remote towns turn to microgrids
item.description: The setup is expected to avoid about 80% of the outages the town previously experienced.

item.description: Emaa Hussen, 34, faces life in prison for allegedly trying to smuggle 320kg of meth hidden in bags of charcoal.

item.title: 100 Colombian guerrillas disarm in deal with leftist government
item.description: About 100 guerrillas laid down their weapons Thursday in a jungle area of southern Colombia under a deal with leftist President Gustavo Petro, who has sought peace with armed rebels, AFP journalists witnessed.The leftist president has angered Washington by refusing to extradite guerrilla commanders committed to the peace process.

item.description: Dispatches from Ukraine. Day 1577.

item.title: Labour's Andy Burnham wins a special election, setting up a showdown with Starmer to lead Britain
item.description: Labour’s Andy Burnham, the current mayor of Greater Manchester, has won a special election for a seat in Parliament that puts him in a position to challenge embattled Prime Minister Keir Starmer for leadership of the country. Burnham decisively won the seat of Makerfield in northwest England over Rob Kenyon of the anti-immigration party Reform UK. The victory announced early Friday cements the status of Burnham, a 56-year-old politician nicknamed the King of the North, as the top contender to replace Starmer as leader of the Labour Party and the country.

item.title: Japan doctor-turned-author moots amputations to ease care crunch
item.description: Like the protagonist in his taboo novel, former doctor-turned-author Yo Kusakabe believes chopping off elderly patients' useless limbs could help prevent a potential collapse of super-ageing Japan's overstressed care industry."Japan's caregiving industry isn't collapsing yet -- but with (the number of) elderly people requiring care predicted to keep increasing, it is heading toward breaking point," Kusakabe said.

item.title: Toronto police make arrest in U.S. consulate shooting
item.description: Toronto police arrested a man who was wanted in a shooting at the U.S. consulate, in addition to allegedly participating in other shootings there.

item.title: UN reports record violations of children in conflict, with government forces the main perpetrators
item.description: Nearly 25,000 children caught in conflict were victims of a record number of violations last year, including killings, rape and recruitment to fight, and for the first time, government forces — not armed groups — were the main perpetrators, a new United Nations report says. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres’ annual report, released this week, has a blacklist of violators against children: government forces from eight nations and 67 armed groups from 16 countries and territories. The number of violations — which also include abductions, attacks on schools and hospitals, and denial of humanitarian access to help them — rose for a fourth straight year to 38,558, according to the report that is based on verified U.N. data.

The number represented ‌the total ⁠number of confirmed cases as of Wednesday, ⁠according to a situation report that documented 21 ​new cases ​and six ​new deaths ‌in the previous 24 hours.

item.title: Greece's Parthenon gets a facelift, revealing a look not seen for 220 years
item.description: Visitors to the Acropolis can now see the Parthenon’s western side looking whole for the first time in about 220 years. Officially unveiled Thursday, restorers have slotted two new marble blocks into long-empty gaps high on the temple’s western end — the view visitors see when they first enter the ancient monument in Athens. “They allow the unique proportions and the geometric perfection of the Parthenon’s western face to be seen once again,” she said.

item.title: Man arrested in UK after toddler ends up in zoo crocodile enclosure
item.description: Police in the UK have arrested a man on suspicion of attempted murder after a 3-year-old boy was found in a critical condition inside a zoo’s crocodile enclosure.

item.title: Jerusalem light rail service partially suspended due to haredi protests
item.description: An additional demonstration is being held near Prison 10 in protest of the arrest of a draft dodger earlier this week. Light rail service has been partially suspended between Jerusalem’s central bus station and Mount Herzl station on Thursday evening as haredi (ultra-Orthodox) protesters began protesting the arrest of a draft dodger earlier this week.

item.title: How Ukraine found a way through Moscow’s air defences
item.description: Hundreds of Ukrainian drones met little resistance as they soared through the Moscow skyline overnight and devastated a major Russian oil refinery.

item.title: Netanyahu rules out Israeli troop withdrawal from southern Lebanon
item.description: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu on Thursday ruled out a withdrawal of Israeli troops from southern Lebanon for the foreseeable future. In a speech, Netanyahu described the "security zone" established by the Israeli military as a barrier between the Hezbollah militia in Lebanon and civilians and communities in northern Israel.

item.description: Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei said in a letter shared by state media on Thursday that he approved the framework agreement with the United States but with reservations. Khamenei said he disagreed in "principle" with the deal but ultimately gave his approval out of a sense of responsibility toward the Iranian people and its allies.

item.title: Peru’s president announces that Pope Leo will visit in early November
item.description: Peruvian President José María Balcázar announced Thursday that Pope Leo XIV will visit Peru in the first half of November. Balcázar said after meeting with the pontiff at the Vatican that Leo will travel to Puno, Iquitos, Cusco, Pucallpa, Piura and Chiclayo, where the Pope carried out his pastoral work for nearly a decade. The Chicago-born pope previously lived in Trujillo, on the northwestern coast, and became a Peruvian citizen in 2015. The Vatican has not confirmed any upcoming trip, but a visit to Peru with possible stops in other South American countries is widely rumored.

item.title: Russia Wants AI Sovereignty. It Has a Chip Problem

item.description: Ghana hosted a landmark global conference on Thursday seeking to translate growing political support for slavery reparations into practical commitments towards justice."The growing international support for these conversations demonstrates that reparatory justice is no longer a peripheral issue," Ghana's foreign minister said.

item.description: Europe risks "total technological irrelevance" if it fails to develop sovereign capabilities, the head of Canadian AI startup Cohere warned Thursday on the sidelines of the Vivatech trade fair in Paris.Since then it has also snapped up Canadian-German biomedical startup Reliant AI. And it signed partnerships in May with Spanish firms Indra, a defence specialist, and Multiverse computing, which develops AI software.

The Major Oak, a centuries-old tree long believed to have sheltered the legendary outlaw Robin Hood, has died, ending a remarkable run that spanned more than […]

item.title: Cuba leader admits 'urgent changes' needed to overcome crisis
item.description: Cuba's communist model needs "urgent changes" to overcome a major crisis which cannot be solely blamed on a crippling US oil blockade, President Miguel Diaz-Canel said in his frankest admission yet of the need for radical reforms.While Havana's impulse has been to blame its woes on a more-than-six-decade US trade embargo and more recent blockade, Diaz-Canel admitted in remarks broadcast Thursday there were "obstacles that don't come from outside, nor the blockade."

item.title: Accused in South African trial admits attack on missing German tourist
item.description: One of the four accused in a case concerning the disappearance of a German holidaymaker in Cape Town has confessed to robbing the tourist of his possessions, a South African court heard on Thursday. In a video clip played to the court in the Cape Town suburb of Wynberg, the man admits to being part of a group that attacked Nick Frischke and his friends on a mountain path in the area.

item.description: Ukraine launched one of its largest drone attacks on Moscow, striking a major oil refinery and sending thick black smoke over parts of the Russian capital.

item.title: Escaped German murderer crashes getaway motorcycle in Italy
item.description: A convicted murderer who escaped while out of prison on accompanied release has been found in an Italian hospital after crashing his getaway motorcycle, German police reported on Thursday. The 42-year-old man escaped on the motorcycle in Peine in the German state of Lower Saxony on Tuesday afternoon.

item.title: Some EU leaders caution against talks with Russia
item.description: BRUSSELS, June 18 () - Several EU leaders said on Thursday there was no point in rushing into talks with Russia after an EU official said on Wednesday the office ‌of European Council President Antonio Costa had made "brief contacts at diplomatic level" with the Kremlin. European leaders ‌have recently stepped up discussions about the possibility of direct talks with Russia over the war in Ukraine after isolating Moscow ​diplomatically since it attacked Ukraine in February 2022.

item.title: Brazilian police probe senator close to Lula
item.description: Brazilian police investigating a banking scandal carried out raids Thursday against targets including a senator allied with President Luiz Inacio Lula de Silva, bringing the affair closer to his government as he seeks reelection in October.A police source said that the targets of these raids included Jaques Wagner, a close ally of Lula and his government's top representative in the Senate with the job of pushing the president's legislative agenda in that chamber.

item.title: South Africa builds another site to ease overcrowding and speed up deportation of Malawian nationals
item.description: South Africa started building a second temporary deportation center on Thursday to process the repatriation of Malawians after thousands protested earlier this year in Johannesburg and other parts of the country against illegal immigration that has fueled tensions between locals and foreign nationals. Meanwhile, thousands more Malawian nationals arrived at the first deportation center, also in the city of Durban, in an area known as Sherwood, where an estimated 10,000 have been camping for more than a week, waiting to be sent home. According to South African officials, at least 12 women had given birth at the site since Malawians started gathering there.

item.title: Pope Leo XIV to visit Peru in November
item.description: Peruvian President José María Balcázar confirmed Wednesday that Pope Leo XIV will visit Peru during the first half of November in a trip expected to last between eight and 10 days.

item.title: Zimbabwean parliament extends President Mnangagwa's term
item.description: The Zimbabwean parliament on Thursday passed a law amending the constitution to extend the presidential term by two years and providing for the president to be elected by parliament rather than by popular vote. The measure, which was backed by 216 of 258 members in the National Assembly, still has to be approved by the country's Senate before being signed into law by President Emmerson Mnangagwa.

item.description: By Olivia Le Poidevin GENEVA, June 18 (Reuters) - Dozens of countries including Britain, France and Germany raised the alarm at the U.N.

item.title: Admiral notes 'increasingly aggressive' Russian presence in the Baltic
item.description: The presence of Russian warships and security in the Baltic Sea region remains a particular focus for the German Navy, fleet commander Vice Admiral Axel Deertz, said on the sidelines of a conference in Kiel in northern Germany on Thursday. “This is an area where the security situation has noticeably intensified, where a potential adversary is quite active and is behaving in an increasingly aggressive manner,” the commander warned at the International Seapower Symposium in Kiel.

item.title: Zimbabwe vote to extend president's term underscores the staying power of Africa's aging leaders
item.description: Zimbabwe lawmakers voted on Thursday for constitutional changes that would defer elections and extend the tenure of the country’s 83-year-old president. The vote underscores the staying power of Africa's aging leaders on a continent that has some of the world’s oldest rulers despite boasting the youngest population globally. Zimbabwe's National Assembly overwhelmingly approved constitutional amendments that would postpone elections due in 2028 to 2030 and extend President Emmerson Mnangagwa’s term by two years.

item.title: Niger says soldiers, civilians killed in armed attack on Niamey airport
item.description: Ministry of Defence says 11 soldiers, 2 civilians and 22 attackers killed in Diori Hamani International Airport assault.

item.title: Lebanon: Tyre residents return to homes shattered by strikes
item.description: Lebanese families returning to Tyre found their homes in ruins on 18 June after Israeli strikes and voiced cautious optimism about a new US-Iran agreement.

item.title: Iran war leaves Islamic republic intact and opponents divided
item.description: The Iran war was cast as a catalyst for the Islamic republic's collapse, but months of fighting failed to dislodge the clerical leadership and left its opponents out in the cold.Rights groups and the United Nations have sounded the alarm over a surge in Iran in executions -- more than 40 since the war started -- and arrests in recent months, including many in connection with the protests the authorities called "terrorist riots".

item.title: French fishermen gather in Cherbourg as sector faces down multiple challenges
item.description: In total, the French government said €13 million would be dedicated to the fishing sector for fuel consumption in April and May.

item.title: Farmers deny charges over cow trampling death
item.description: Rebekah Morris, 29, was trampled to death by a cow while walking her dog Zero in 2022.

item.title: Maritime data company says stranded ships have begun transiting the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Major shipowners have begun moving vessels through the Strait of Hormuz since the U.S. and Iran signed an interim agreement to end their war, maritime data company Lloyd’s List Intelligence said Thursday. In a media briefing, Richard Meade, editor in chief of Lloyd’s List, said for the first time in 110 days, ships owned by major companies were crossing the strait after effectively being marooned there since February. Before the war, the waterway off Iran's coast carried a fifth of the world’s crude oil.

item.title: Foreign aid cuts push up migrant flows, IOM chief warns
item.description: Cuts in development aid by wealthy countries tend to drive up displacement away from the world's poorest regions, the head of the UN's International Organization for Migration warned in an interview with AFP on Thursday.Several rich Western countries -- particularly the United States but also many European nations -- have cut their development aid budgets in recent years, while also tightening migration policies and strengthening border controls.

item.title: Admiral notes 'increasingly aggressive' Russian presence in the Baltic
item.description: The presence of Russian warships and security in the Baltic Sea region remains a particular focus for the German Navy, fleet commander Vice-Admiral Axel Deertz, said on the sidelines of a conference in Kiel in northern Germany on Thursday. “This is an area where the security situation has noticeably intensified, where a potential adversary is quite active and is behaving in an increasingly aggressive manner,” the commander warned at the International Seapower Symposium in Kiel.

item.title: 'Do not travel' advice for Dubai dropped
item.description: Thousands of Brits were left stranded in the Middle East when the US-Iran war broke out in early 2026.

item.title: Ebola cases increase almost 40% in a week as death toll passes 200
item.description: The Ebola outbreak in Congo and Uganda has claimed more than 200 lives in its first month and is the worst known outbreak at this stage, with up to 35,000 suspected potential contacts, Africa’s Centres for Disease Control and Prevention said on Thursday. With 894 confirmed cases so far, the current outbreak is three times worse than a previous outbreak in Uganda in 2000, which had 281 cases at the same point, said Dr. Wessam Mankoula, a medical epidemiologist at Africa CDC. The number of cases has increased 38% since last week and is now in 32 health zones across eastern Congo, said Mankoula.

item.title: Inquest Into Influencer Stacey Warnecke’s ‘Freebirth’ Death Comes to a Halt as Critical New Evidence Emerges: Report
item.description: The content creator died on Sept. 29, hours after giving birth to her first son

item.title: Girl, 5, and Her Brother, 1, Fatally Struck by SUV While Walking Home from School with Their Mom
item.description: "I wish it hit me, not my babies," the children's mother said after the deadly crash

item.title: Polish police arrest suspect in killing of exiled Russian artist
item.description: Polish police have arrested a suspect in the fatal shooting of an exiled Russian artist and Kremlin critic, four days after the attack, authorities said on Thursday. Investigators are still examining whether anyone assisted the suspect in planning the killing or helping him flee, a spokesman for police in the eastern Lublin region said.

item.title: Italy lacks evidence for now to seek trial in Sarajevo sniper probe, source says
item.description: MILAN, June 18 () - Italian prosecutors investigating accusations of "sniper tourism" in 1990s Sarajevo do not currently have enough evidence to seek a trial for any suspects, a source with direct ‌knowledge of the matter said on Thursday. Milan prosecutors have placed five people under investigation over ‌allegations that Italians and other foreigners paid to shoot at civilians during the 1992-95 siege of Sarajevo, the source said.

item.title: Ashes of Missing College Student Found Dead in Japan Will Be Scattered ‘Right Where He Wants to Be,’ Says Reverend
item.description: Two benches in Weston Higginbotham’s honor will also be installed at Oak Mountain State Park in Alabama

item.title: Keeping up appearances: Greece reveals Parthenon façade after 220 years
item.description: For the first time in about 220 years, the west façade of the Parthenon in Greece can be see in its fullest possible form. External scaffolding has now been permanently removed from the monument, marking a key step in its long restoration.View on euronews

item.title: US family returns four artifacts to Egypt, including statue head thought to depict female pharoah
item.description: The artifacts were received in Cairo on June 5 before being transferred to the Egyptian Museum for further examination, documentation and restoration ahead of their display in the museum. Four ancient artifacts were returned to Egypt by an American family in early June, announced the Egyptian Tourism and Antiquities Ministry.

item.title: Oil tankers pass Hormuz Strait after war deal: tracker
item.description: Three big Saudi oil tankers left the Gulf through the Hormuz Strait on Thursday, maritime trackers said, after leaders vowed to reopen the route under an agreement to end the US-Iran war.Among them were three very large crude carriers (VLCC), each transporting two million barrels of oil from Saudi Arabia, the tracker said.

item.title: Zimbabwe lawmakers back legislation extending president's time in power
item.description: By Chris Takudzwa Muronzi and Nelson Banya HARARE, June 18 (Reuters) - Zimbabwe's lower house of parliament passed a bill to extend presidential terms from five to seven years on Thursday, which would

item.title: Why fines for going topless are becoming more common in France’s holiday destinations
item.description: While being topless on beaches are still allowed, authorities are clamping down on those wandering around town centres without a shirt

item.title: Israeli attacks kill three people in southern Lebanon
item.description: At least three people have been killed in renewed Israeli attacks in southern Lebanon, Lebanese reports said on Thursday. Two people were killed in an Israeli drone strike on a town south-east of Nabatiyeh in the south, the state news agency NNA reported.

item.title: Ukraine and Russia again exchange bodies of fallen
item.description: Moscow and Kiev have again exchanged bodies of the fallen in their four-year war, the Ukrainian authorities reported on Thursday. Ukraine received 522 bodies.

item.title: Western façade of Parthenon restored after 200-year project
item.description: The restoration of the western façade of the Parthenon has been completed, marking the culmination of a project that has spanned more than two centuries, Greek Culture Minister Lina Mendoni said on Thursday. Scaffolding on the western side of the ancient temple on the Acropolis in Athens has now been removed, revealing the western façade for the first time in more than 200 years.

item.title: Russian nuclear chief accuses Ukraine of killing worker at Zaporizhzhia power plant in drone attack
item.description: MOSCOW, June 18 () - The head of Russia's state nuclear corporation Rosatom on Thursday ‌accused Ukraine of killing a key worker ‌in a drone attack on the Russian-controlled Zaporizhzhia nuclear power ​plant in southeastern Ukraine. There was no immediate response from Ukraine and could not independently verify the statement.

item.title: Novelist Kundera and wife buried in Czech home city
item.description: The remains of Czech-French novelist Milan Kundera and his wife Vera were buried in the writer's native city of Brno on Thursday, the city hall said."The burial was held in a narrow circle of family members, friends, French embassy staff and representatives of Brno, a city of which Kundera was an honorary citizen," the Brno city hall said on its website.

item.title: He Wanted to Study Business. He Ended Up Romance-Scamming Older Women. He Told Me Everything.
item.description: First, it was the Nigerian prince. Then the distressed soldier. It’s gotten even worse.
